topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop and deploy ultra-low-latency, lightweight online learning quantum error correction controllers that adapt in real-time to dynamic noise fluctuations on NISQ devices, leveraging streaming quantum measurement data to optimize error correction with minimal qubit overhead.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction (QEC) methods for NISQ devices suffer from prohibitive qubit overhead, static error models, and offline or high-latency inference that prevent real-time adaptation to rapidly changing noise environments. Existing ML-based error mitigation techniques operate offline or with high latency, limiting their ability to respond to transient noise dynamics that degrade quantum control fidelity during computation. The open problem is to create QEC schemes that can perform real-time error detection and correction with minimal resource overhead while adapting online to the temporal noise variations inherent in NISQ hardware.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel real-time quantum control system integrating a lightweight, streaming-capable machine learning model (e.g., a quantized recurrent neural network or spiking neural network) trained online to predict error syndromes and optimize QEC code parameters dynamically. This system will ingest noisy stabilizer measurement outcomes continuously, update its model weights with low computational overhead, and trigger adaptive error correction operations within the strict timing constraints of quantum gate cycles. The QEC framework will combine low-overhead codes (e.g., tailored variants of the rotated surface code or subsystem codes) with the ML controller to minimize logical error rates while respecting NISQ qubit and latency constraints.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on IBM Quantum Experience's public NISQ backends (e.g., ibmq_mumbai, ibmq_lagos) and the Rigetti Aspen-11 device, using the Qiskit Ignis dataset and the Rigetti Forest noise characterization dataset for benchmarking. Baselines include the Low-Overhead QEC Codes (2026), traditional Surface Code QEC, and the Real-Time Quantum Control Systems model described above. Metrics will be logical error rate reduction (>30% improvement over static QEC baselines), inference latency (<1 µs per inference cycle), and qubit overhead reduction (≤40% fewer qubits than surface code). Experiments include ablation over model architectures, QEC codes, and online vs. offline training, conducted over 10 independent runs with statistical significance testing.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior low-overhead QEC codes that operate on static error models and offline error mitigation systems that cannot meet real-time constraints, this work introduces a dynamically adaptive, online-trained ML controller embedded within the quantum control loop, enabling real-time syndrome prediction and correction with provably lower latency and resource overhead on NISQ devices. This capability to adapt to time-varying noise in situ during quantum operations is unprecedented in current QEC frameworks.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a lightweight, ultra-low latency machine learning inference framework, QuantumQuickCorrect (QQC), that enables real-time quantum error correction on NISQ devices by integrating optimized spatiotemporal error prediction with hardware-accelerated model deployment, achieving immediate in-sequence error mitigation during fast quantum gate operations.

BACKGROUND:  
Current real-time quantum control systems fail to implement effective error correction within the strict timing constraints of rapid quantum gate sequences in NISQ devices due to the computational complexity and latency of existing machine learning-based QEC approaches. This bottleneck prevents on-the-fly error correction, limiting quantum circuit fidelity and operational stability during fast quantum operations. Thus, a dedicated solution balancing high accuracy and minimal inference latency is crucial for practical QEC deployment in near-term quantum hardware.

APPROACH:  
QuantumQuickCorrect (QQC) designs a compact neural architecture combining spatiotemporal convolutional layers with quantized recurrent units to predict error syndromes directly from partial syndrome measurement data streams, enabling ultra-fast inference compatible with gate timescales. The model is co-optimized with FPGA-based low-level hardware acceleration tailored for NISQ device control electronics to guarantee inference latency below gate operation durations (~100 ns). Additionally, QQC incorporates adaptive pruning and dynamic precision scaling to further reduce computational overhead while maintaining logical error rate reductions.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate QQC on three benchmark quantum error syndrome datasets: the IBM Quantum Experience Surface-17 Syndrome Dataset, Google Sycamore Noise Model Data, and Rigetti Aspen-9 Device Error Logs. Baselines include the ML decoders from Bonet-Monroig et al. (2021), the Real-Time Decoder by Baireuther et al. (2018), and the Lightweight Neural Decoder (LND) from Torlai et al. (2022). Metrics include syndrome decoding accuracy (>95%), logical error rate reduction (>30% improvement over baselines), and inference latency (target <100 ns, reducing latency by at least 50% compared to the fastest baseline). Experiments run on FPGA-equipped NISQ control units with a compute budget of 48 GPU-hours for model training and 30 repeated runs per setting for statistical significance; ablation studies will assess effects of pruning, quantization, and hardware co-design.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior ML-based QEC decoders that prioritize accuracy at the cost of high latency or computational intensity, QQC uniquely integrates model architecture design with hardware acceleration to meet the stringent timing demands of real-time quantum gate sequences on NISQ devices, enabling immediate, in-circuit error correction unattainable by current state-of-the-art decoders.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop a low-latency, adaptive quantum error correction decoder that integrates real-time noise characterization with dynamic code parameter adjustment on NISQ devices to significantly reduce logical error rates under fluctuating noise environments.

BACKGROUND: NISQ devices suffer from rapidly time-varying noise and gate errors that degrade quantum computation fidelity. Existing quantum error correction schemes, including state-of-the-art low-overhead codes and adaptive decoders, cannot update decoding parameters quickly enough (under 1 ms) to track transient noise fluctuations, resulting in suboptimal error suppression and limiting practical NISQ algorithm execution. The open problem is achieving real-time, low-latency adaptive decoding that can integrate streaming measurement data to continuously optimize error correction on NISQ hardware.

APPROACH: We propose a novel adaptive quantum error correction decoder architecture that fuses streaming syndrome measurement data with an online noise estimation module using lightweight Bayesian inference, enabling sub-millisecond updates to decoding parameters such as syndrome weighting and code stabilizer configurations. This decoder leverages a hybrid classical-quantum pipeline optimized for low overhead on NISQ hardware, dynamically tuning low-overhead quantum error correction codes (e.g., tailored variants of surface or Bacon-Shor codes) to changing noise profiles. The system continuously adapts via a feedback loop incorporating quantum sensor outputs and gate error rates, maintaining optimal logical qubit fidelity in real time.

EXPERIMENT: Validate the approach on three benchmark quantum error correction datasets: (1) the QEC Benchmark Suite (QEC-Bench 2023) for surface code syndrome data under fluctuating noise, (2) IBM’s Qiskit Aer noise model streaming datasets simulating NISQ device outputs, and (3) the Google Sycamore error syndrome logs with time-varying error profiles. Baselines include: the fixed-parameter Surface Code decoder (MWPM), the low-overhead code from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” (2026), and the “Low-latency adaptive decoding for real-time quantum sensors” decoder. Metrics for evaluation include logical error rate reduction (>30% absolute improvement over fixed decoders), decoder latency (<1 ms update time), and circuit fidelity (>15% increase on noisy quantum circuits). Experiments will be conducted on a hybrid classical-quantum simulator with compute resources equivalent to 100 CPU cores over 50 independent noise seeds; ablation studies will isolate contributions of Bayesian noise estimation and code parameter adaptation.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior adaptive decoders that either operate offline or fail to meet sub-millisecond latency requirements, this method achieves real-time continuous code parameter tuning informed by streaming noise estimation, enabling robust error correction in the highly dynamic noise environments specific to NISQ devices. It meaningfully surpasses static and prior adaptive decoders by integrating online Bayesian noise inference with dynamic code adaptation on low-overhead codes, a combination not previously demonstrated.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop a noise-adaptive, dynamically reconfigurable quantum error correction and compiler co-design framework that optimizes QEC code parameters and gate scheduling in real-time to maximize fault tolerance and throughput on NISQ quantum batch servers.

BACKGROUND: Current quantum error correction (QEC) methods for NISQ devices either incur prohibitive qubit overhead or fail to adapt to time-varying noise and gate error fluctuations in batch quantum jobs, limiting reliable computation and throughput. Existing compilers do not dynamically integrate real-time noise profiles with flexible QEC parameter tuning, causing suboptimal logical error rates and underutilized hardware in noisy, intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) batch environments.

APPROACH: We propose an adaptive QEC-compiler co-design system that leverages continuous noise monitoring to dynamically select and parameterize low-overhead QEC codes (e.g., small-block stabilizer codes or tailored surface code variants) and schedules gates to minimize logical errors over extended batch workloads. This system couples a real-time noise profiler with a QEC code parameter tuner and gate-level scheduler to optimize both error correction strength and circuit latency on-the-fly, thus enhancing fault tolerance while maximizing job throughput on batch quantum servers.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on quantum workload benchmarks including IBM Quantum Challenge circuits, Google Sycamore random circuits, and Quantum Volume 64 tests, comparing against baselines such as static surface code error correction, “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” (2026), and “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” (2026). Metrics include logical error rate reduction (>30% vs. static QEC), throughput increase (>20% job completion rate improvement), and gate scheduling latency reduction (>15%). Experiments will run on IBM Qiskit Aer simulator calibrated with real IBM Quantum hardware noise profiles, across 10 random seeds, including ablation studies disabling adaptive tuning and noise profiling.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that statically applies QEC codes or only optimizes compilers for fixed noise assumptions, this approach jointly adapts QEC parameters and gate scheduling dynamically to real-time noise fluctuations in batch contexts, enabling significantly improved fault tolerance and throughput on NISQ devices with minimal qubit overhead.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight, dynamically adaptive quantum error correction (QEC) framework integrated into ultra-compact noise-aware embedded quantum compilers that operate with minimal classical overhead on NISQ devices.

BACKGROUND: Current quantum error correction codes are either too resource-intensive or too static to efficiently support NISQ devices, which have stringent qubit and classical processing limitations. Existing compilers fail to embed detailed noise models dynamically, preventing effective real-time error mitigation in embedded control systems. This bottleneck severely restricts quantum fidelity and scalability in practical NISQ hardware deployments.

APPROACH: We propose an integrated embedded quantum control system that combines a noise-aware compilation pipeline with an adaptive QEC code selector and updater module. The compiler incorporates lightweight, parameterized noise models updated in situ from real-time device telemetry, enabling dynamic tailoring of low-overhead QEC codes (such as variants of the [[4,2,2]] code or tailored stabilizer codes) and gate sequences. This system is optimized for ultra-low-latency classical processing environments proximal to the quantum hardware, enabling continuous error mitigation with minimal overhead.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate the framework on IBM Quantum Experience backends and Rigetti Aspen devices using benchmark quantum circuits from the Quantum Volume (QV) suite, the QAOA for MaxCut problems on 6-10 qubits, and the GHZ state preparation protocol. Metrics will include logical error rates (target <0.005 logical error per gate), compilation latency reduction (>40% vs Qiskit noise-aware compilation), and gate fidelity improvements (>15% absolute increase vs IBM Qiskit’s baseline). Experiments will run with 5 random seeds per benchmark and include ablations on noise model complexity and QEC adaptation frequency.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work such as “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” or static noise-aware compilers, this method uniquely integrates adaptive QEC code selection tightly coupled with ultra-compact embedded compilers capable of continuous noise model updates in severely resource-constrained environments. It directly addresses the critical and previously unresolved bottleneck of real-time dynamic QEC adaptation within embedded quantum control systems.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hybrid generative model integrating stochastic latent dynamics with physics-informed molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to predict temporally resolved protein folding pathways and intermediate states beyond static final structures.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art methods like AlphaFold accurately predict native folded protein structures but do not model the dynamic folding process, missing critical transient intermediates and folding kinetics. The open problem is the lack of computational frameworks that combine data-driven generative modeling with time-dependent physical constraints to reconstruct realistic folding trajectories, which are essential for understanding folding mechanisms, misfolding diseases, and rational protein design.

APPROACH:  
We propose a two-stage framework: first, leverage high-resolution MD simulation datasets to train a physics-informed variational recurrent neural network (PI-VRNN) that learns stochastic latent representations of folding kinetics constrained by energy landscapes and structural physics. Second, integrate this model with a generative diffusion process conditioned on intermediate MD snapshots to produce coherent temporal folding trajectories, capturing folding intermediates and transition states with spatial and temporal fidelity. This approach explicitly models folding as a time-dependent stochastic process constrained by physical laws, unlike static predictions.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on multiple benchmark MD trajectory datasets such as Dynameomics, Folding@home protein folding trajectories, and the Shaw Research long-timescale folding dataset. Baselines include AlphaFold2, RoseTTAFold, and DeepFragLib fragment-based predictions. Quantitatively assess temporal accuracy using root-mean-square deviation (RMSD) trajectories (targeting >15% reduction in RMSD error versus baselines on intermediate states), folding pathway fidelity via transition path sampling metrics (aiming for >10% improvement), and folding kinetics reconstruction using folding rate correlation (>0.7 Pearson correlation with experimental rates). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds for stability; ablations will isolate physics-informed loss terms and latent stochasticity contributions.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work such as AlphaFold and DeepFragLib that predict only final folded structures or static fragments, our method explicitly models the folding process as a time-dependent stochastic dynamical system constrained by physical energies, enabling generation of realistic folding trajectories and folding intermediates for the first time with comparable accuracy to high-resolution MD simulations but vastly improved computational efficiency.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a multi-objective neural architecture search (NAS) framework that jointly optimizes protein structure prediction accuracy, energy efficiency, and model robustness on edge GPUs, enabling real-time, battery-powered deployment of AlphaFold-like models for field applications.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art protein structure prediction models, including AlphaFold2 and its derivatives, achieve remarkable accuracy but require substantial computational resources and power, making real-time inference on battery-powered edge devices infeasible. Existing NAS approaches for protein folding focus predominantly on accuracy or latency, neglecting the crucial factor of energy consumption and robustness under noisy or incomplete input data typical in field scenarios. Addressing this limitation is critical for deploying protein folding models in portable, low-power environments for continuous biological monitoring, such as environmental biosensing or on-site drug discovery.

APPROACH:  
We propose an energy-robust neural architecture search that integrates three core objectives: minimizing energy consumption per inference, maximizing prediction accuracy (measured by TM-score and lDDT), and enhancing robustness against input noise and low-resolution data. Our NAS method incorporates energy profiling on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX and Orin edge GPUs during the search loop, alongside robustness evaluation using input perturbations derived from low-resolution Cryo-EM maps and sparse Multiple Sequence Alignments (MSAs). The search space includes lightweight transformer adaptations, quantization-aware components, and conditional computation modules tailored for protein folding tasks. Final model candidates will be fine-tuned on diverse protein datasets to ensure generalizability.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark on ProteinNet (CASP13, CASP14 subsets), CAMEO continuous evaluation benchmark, and the Low-Resolution Cryo-EM Map Dataset from EMDB. Baselines include AlphaFold2, OpenFold, and trRosetta. Evaluation metrics include TM-score (targeting ≤3% accuracy drop vs. AlphaFold2), lDDT score, energy consumption per inference (aiming for >30% reduction measured in joules on NVIDIA Jetson devices), and robustness quantified by accuracy degradation under simulated noise (target ≤5% accuracy loss). Experimental protocol involves training under a fixed compute budget of 500 GPU hours using NVIDIA A100 nodes for NAS search and fine-tuning, with at least 5 random seeds for statistical significance and ablation studies isolating energy optimization and robustness objectives.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which either optimizes for accuracy alone or latency without energy considerations, this research explicitly integrates energy consumption and robustness as first-class objectives within the NAS loop for protein folding models on edge GPUs. This enables discovering models that balance accuracy, power efficiency, and resilience to noisy inputs—capabilities absent from AlphaFold derivatives and recent NAS efforts—unlocking practical, real-time protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold’s current deployment scope.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Develop a physics-informed, ultra-compact generative neural model for real-time protein folding prediction deployable on edge devices, balancing inference speed and memory footprint while strictly enforcing biophysical constraints.

BACKGROUND: Current state-of-the-art protein structure predictors like AlphaFold2 require massive computational resources and specialized hardware, precluding their use in real-time, in situ applications on resource-constrained edge devices such as portable sequencers or field-deployable diagnostic tools. Existing edge-focused models sacrifice physical accuracy or rely on post hoc refinement, limiting reliability in critical scenarios requiring immediate feedback. The open problem is achieving accurate, biophysically consistent protein folding predictions in real-time on hardware with strict memory (<1 GB) and compute (single embedded GPU/CPU) budgets.

APPROACH: We propose a hybrid physics-informed generative model architecture that integrates lightweight graph neural networks with differentiable biophysical energy constraints embedded as loss function terms, enabling direct generation of protein backbone and sidechain conformations. The model is optimized via neural architecture search for minimal parameter count and inference latency while maintaining native-like folding accuracy. Quantization-aware training and model pruning techniques compress the model further for edge deployment. A modular pipeline performs end-to-end folding from sequence without reliance on heavy multiple sequence alignments or large precomputed embeddings, facilitating rapid prediction under one second per protein on ARM-based edge GPUs.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate performance on CASP14 official targets, CAMEO continuous evaluation dataset, and the ProteinNet dataset to benchmark structure accuracy and generalizability. Baselines include AlphaFold2, ESMFold, and OmegaFold. Metrics include TM-score (target >0.7), RMSD (target <3.5 Å), inference latency on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX (target <1 s per protein), and memory footprint (target <1 GB). Experiments will be run with 5 random seeds to ensure reproducibility, and ablation studies will analyze the impact of physics-informed constraints and architecture components.

NOVELTY: Unlike AlphaFold2 and related models that require extensive compute and memory resources, this approach uniquely combines physics-based constraints directly into a generative model architecture specifically optimized for real-time edge inference. Prior edge or compact models do not simultaneously guarantee strict biophysical validity nor achieve sub-second prediction times on embedded hardware with <1 GB RAM, making this the first deployable, physics-respecting real-time protein folding model for point-of-care and field applications.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Function-Constrained Generative Transformer that integrates differentiable catalytic-site activity modules directly into sequence-structure co-design, enabling end-to-end optimization of industrial enzyme variants with guaranteed folding accuracy and enhanced catalytic performance across diverse chemical environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Current protein design and prediction models, including state-of-the-art generative frameworks, reliably produce sequences that fold into stable structures but fail to enforce functional catalytic constraints, resulting in enzymes that do not meet industrial activity requirements. While AlphaFold and its successors excel at structure prediction, they lack explicit integration of functional optimization, and recent models like Function-Constrained Generative Models have yet to demonstrate robust multi-condition catalytic performance or joint sequence-structure design. The open problem is to develop a unified model that simultaneously optimizes enzyme structure fidelity and catalytic function under variable pH and temperature conditions, a capability absent from current approaches.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel Transformer-based generative model architecture that combines a protein sequence generator with an integrated differentiable catalytic activity predictor and structural fidelity module. This model incorporates explicit catalytic-site constraints derived from experimentally validated active-site residue motifs and reaction mechanism features, encoded as differentiable loss functions within the training objective. The training pipeline jointly optimizes sequence embedding generation, 3D folding prediction (leveraging a modified AlphaFold backbone), and a differentiable enzymatic activity surrogate trained on kinetic parameter data, enabling direct gradient-based optimization of catalytic efficiency alongside structural RMSD. Chemical condition robustness is modeled via multi-task conditioning layers representing pH and temperature, allowing the model to generate enzyme variants tailored for specific industrial environments.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate the model on the following datasets: (1) BRENDA enzyme kinetics database for catalytic parameter benchmarking, (2) FireProtDB for thermostability and mutational data, and (3) the Enzyme Structure-Function Benchmark dataset (ESFB) containing enzyme sequences with measured catalytic efficiencies under varying conditions. Baselines include AlphaFold2, ProteinMPNN, and the current Function-Constrained Generative Models approach. Metrics are catalytic efficiency improvement (kcat/KM) with an expectation of >15% absolute gain, structural fidelity with RMSD <2.5Å against experimentally determined structures, and robustness quantified by <10% variance in catalytic performance across pH 5-9 and temperatures 25°C-60°C. The experimental protocol involves training on NVIDIA A100 clusters with a 4-week compute budget, running 5 independent seeds for statistical validation, and conducting ablation studies isolating catalytic constraint modules and multi-condition conditioning layers.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that treats folding and activity prediction as separate or sequential tasks, our approach tightly integrates catalytic function and folding into a single differentiable generative framework, enabling direct optimization of enzymatic activity within structural context. The explicit multi-condition robustness modeling and end-to-end gradient-based function-structure co-optimization represent capabilities not achievable by existing models such as AlphaFold or standalone function-constrained generators.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a spatiotemporal graph neural network framework that integrates multi-modal time-resolved NMR, HDX-MS, and cross-linking mass spectrometry data to predict residue-specific protein conformational transitions and dynamic allosteric pathways beyond static structure models.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art methods including AlphaFold and its derivatives excel in predicting static, single-conformation protein structures but lack the capability to resolve detailed residue-level dynamics crucial for understanding functional transitions and drug targeting. Existing dynamic structure prediction approaches, such as those using graph neural networks on time-resolved NMR and cross-linking data, remain limited in input modality scope and temporal modeling granularity, failing to fully capture complex conformational ensembles and their temporal evolution.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-modal spatiotemporal graph neural network (ST-GNN) that encodes protein residues as nodes connected by edges representing both physical proximity and experimentally derived cross-links. Temporal dynamics are modeled explicitly using a combination of temporal attention and recurrent graph convolution layers to learn residue-level flexibility and conformational state transitions over time. The model incorporates integrated datasets from time-resolved NMR chemical shift perturbations, hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS), and cross-linking mass spectrometry (XL-MS) to enrich spatial and temporal features. Training leverages supervised learning on labeled dynamic protein datasets, predicting residue-resolved conformational transition probabilities and identifying allosteric hotspots.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will use three benchmark datasets: (1) the Calmodulin time-resolved NMR and HDX-MS dataset, (2) Ubiquitin dynamic cross-linking dataset, and (3) the ENCORE (Ensemble Characterization of Residue-level Conformations) dataset combining multi-modal dynamic measurements. Baselines include AlphaFold2, Graph Neural Networks for Protein Dynamics (GNPD, the tournament winner), and DeepTracer-LowResEnhance. Metrics include per-residue transition prediction accuracy (>5% absolute improvement over GNPD), F1-score for flexible hotspot identification (>0.75 target), and dynamic cross-link frequency correlation (Pearson’s r > 0.85). The protocol includes training on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~20,000 GPU hours, five independent runs for statistical robustness, and ablation studies analyzing each modality’s contribution and temporal model components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing methods which predict static structures or rely on single experimental modalities, this approach models residue-level temporal dynamics via an explicit spatiotemporal GNN architecture integrating three complementary experimental techniques. It uniquely predicts dynamic allosteric pathways and conformational ensembles with higher granularity and accuracy, surpassing state-of-the-art baselines limited to static or less temporally resolved predictions.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a dynamic, learned routing framework that optimally composes domain-specialized expert modules within large language models based on query context and evolving enterprise workflows, jointly guided by scaling laws that predict module efficiency and performance trade-offs at different model and dataset sizes.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing modular LLM architectures either employ static or heuristic routing among knowledge-specialized modules, resulting in inefficiencies and suboptimal response relevance, especially in complex, multi-domain enterprise workflows. Current scaling law research focuses on monolithic or loosely modular models and does not address dynamic integration of heterogeneous knowledge units under compute and latency constraints. The open problem is how to scale and route specialized LLM modules dynamically to maximize downstream task relevance and efficiency while adhering to strict latency budgets in real-world enterprise environments.

APPROACH:  
We propose a scalable Dynamic Routing of Specialized Knowledge Modules (DR-SKM) mechanism that uses a learned policy network trained to select and compose relevant domain-expert modules conditioned on the query context and live workflow metadata. This routing policy is explicitly regularized by newly derived scaling laws that relate module size, dataset domain diversity, and routing overhead to downstream performance gains, enabling adaptive module pruning or expansion at runtime. The system integrates continual learning to adapt routing policies as workflows and data distributions evolve, minimizing latency growth by constraining routing complexity under 200ms. The routing policy is implemented as a lightweight transformer conditioned on query embeddings and workflow signals, jointly optimized with downstream relevance metrics.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate DR-SKM on multi-domain enterprise datasets including the MultiWOZ 2.4 dialog dataset, the TREC Complex Answer Retrieval dataset, and the public OpenBookQA knowledge-intensive benchmark, comparing against GPT-4 with heuristic routing, MoE Transformer baselines, and RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) models. Metrics include a +15% absolute improvement in domain relevance F1 score, routing latency under 200ms (measured on NVIDIA A100 GPUs), and module utilization efficiency (measured as percentage of modules engaged per query). Ablations will test scaling law-driven routing versus naïve routing and fixed modular baselines over 5 random seeds.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior scaling law studies that analyze monolithic LLMs or static modular architectures, DR-SKM explicitly couples learned dynamic routing policies with theoretical scaling laws to optimize modular composition in real-time workflows. This method enables adaptive, fine-grained integration of heterogeneous knowledge modules beyond static heuristics, achieving measurable gains in relevance and efficiency in complex, multi-domain enterprise scenarios, a capability absent from existing approaches.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: Develop adaptive scaling laws that dynamically optimize large language model capacity, latency, energy, and memory trade-offs for on-device inference under strict edge constraints using a unified framework combining dynamic pruning, architecture slimming, and resource-aware model adaptation.

BACKGROUND: Current scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) primarily focus on training compute, model size, and data efficiency but do not explicitly address the combined constraints of latency, energy consumption, and memory limitations critical for deployment on edge devices such as smartphones and IoT hardware. This lack of integrated scaling guidance limits practical on-device LLM deployment, where inference must be both fast and energy-efficient without sacrificing too much performance.

APPROACH: We propose a novel adaptive model scaling framework that integrates dynamic pruning schedules and architecture slimming conditioned on real-time resource monitoring (e.g., available memory, compute cycles, battery level). By formulating resource-constrained inference as an optimization problem, we derive new scaling laws that predict optimal model configurations (e.g., layer width, depth, sparsity) balancing performance and resource usage. The framework incorporates hardware-aware latency and energy models to guide on-the-fly adaptation of the model during inference.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on GLUE and SuperGLUE benchmarks for general language understanding, and SQuAD 2.0 for question answering, using BERT-base, GPT-2 medium, and MobileBERT as baselines. Measure latency (ms per inference), energy consumption (mJ per query on ARM Cortex-A processors), memory footprint (MB), and task accuracy/F1. Target >25% latency reduction and >20% energy savings at less than 5% accuracy degradation relative to static baseline models. Conduct ablation studies on pruning aggressiveness, slimming granularity, and resource feedback mechanisms, training with a compute budget equivalent to 500 A100-GPU hours and running experiments with 5 random seeds for statistical reliability.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing scaling laws that focus on model size and training efficiency alone, this approach delivers the first unified, quantitative scaling laws explicitly balancing latency, energy, and memory constraints for adaptive on-device LLM inference. Prior work does not provide actionable guidance for dynamic model adaptation under real-time edge constraints, whereas this method enables practical, resource-aware model scaling that can be adjusted on-the-fly during inference.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop dynamic, real-time scaling laws and adaptive model compression techniques for Large Language Models (LLMs) deployed on edge devices, using latency and power consumption as explicit triggers to aggressively scale model size without sacrificing critical downstream task performance.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for LLMs primarily focus on cloud or large-scale training environments, ignoring strict memory, latency, and energy constraints inherent to edge and embedded systems (e.g., mobile phones, IoT devices). The open problem is how to maintain acceptable accuracy and responsiveness of LLMs under stringent, device-specific power and latency budgets, where model scaling decisions must be made dynamically at runtime. Existing methods do not provide principled, real-time performance metrics tied to hardware constraints to guide scaling.

APPROACH:  
We propose a framework that integrates hardware-aware profiling with a lightweight monitoring system to continuously track latency and power consumption metrics on edge devices. Using these runtime signals, the LLM dynamically switches between multiple compressed model configurations (e.g., via structured pruning, quantization, or early exit mechanisms) governed by newly derived scaling laws that relate model size to energy and latency budgets and downstream accuracy on edge-relevant NLP tasks. The framework employs an adaptive controller trained on multi-objective optimization (accuracy vs. latency vs. power) that learns device-specific scaling policies in a data-driven manner.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on GLUE/SuperGLUE benchmarks and the SQuAD 2.0 dataset to measure downstream task performance, alongside latency and power consumption profiling on Nvidia Jetson Xavier NX and Qualcomm Snapdragon 8cx edge platforms. Baselines include BERT-base, DistilBERT, and MobileBERT. Metrics: achieve >15% reduction in latency and >20% reduction in energy use compared to MobileBERT without >3% absolute drop in F1 (SQuAD) or accuracy (GLUE). Ablations include evaluating different scaling triggers and compression methods. Experiments run with fixed 24-hour compute budget, at least 5 random seeds, and cross-validate scaling policies for generalization.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior scaling laws focused on static large-scale training or zero-shot performance, this work explicitly incorporates real-time hardware constraints as triggers for dynamic scaling on edge devices, producing principled, device-tailored scaling laws. This approach enables aggressive, runtime model adaptation maintaining critical task performance under hard latency and power budgets, which existing static scaling laws and cloud-oriented methods cannot address.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: Develop an adaptive, multi-modal noise-resilient filtering framework for web-scale pretraining datasets that dynamically integrates semantic, statistical, and embedding-based redundancy signals with model feedback across scaling phases to optimize large language model (LLM) scaling efficiency and downstream generalization.

BACKGROUND: Current scaling laws for LLMs degrade significantly when pretraining data contains noise, duplicates, or low-quality samples, limiting gains despite increasing dataset size. Existing noise filtering methods such as the winning “Noise-Resilient Dataset Filtering for Web-Scale Pretraining” rely on heuristic thresholds combining statistical and semantic similarity metrics but do not integrate multimodal or embedding-based signals nor dynamically adapt filtering strategies holistically across model scale and training phase. This leaves an open problem in robustly maintaining data diversity and quality to sustain or improve scaling laws in large-scale settings.

APPROACH: Extend prior noise filtering by introducing an adaptive multi-modal filtering pipeline that fuses semantic textual similarity, statistical n-gram overlap, and dense embedding-based redundancy detection from pretrained sentence encoders (e.g., Sentence-BERT). The method dynamically adjusts filtering thresholds via a reinforcement learning loop that uses intermediate LLM training feedback signals (validation loss trends, perplexity on held-out data, and zero-shot task accuracy) to balance between removing noise and preserving diverse data. The filtering pipeline operates in multi-stages, progressively tightening or relaxing filters as the model scales, with a feedback controller trained to optimize scaling efficiency (accuracy at fixed compute budget). Additionally, incorporate a small-scale human-verified corpus to calibrate and validate noise detection.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on Common Crawl (CCNet) datasets with filtering applied at three scaling phases of LLM pretraining (e.g., 1B, 10B, 100B parameter models). Use GLUE, SuperGLUE, and SQuAD 2.0 benchmarks for zero-shot and few-shot downstream evaluation. Baselines include BERT-base, GPT-3 (175B), and the original Noise-Resilient Dataset Filtering method. Metrics: achieve >3% absolute SuperGLUE accuracy improvement over baseline filtering at fixed dataset size; reduce perplexity on held-out CCNet validation splits by >5 points relative to unfiltered data; and demonstrate >30% reduction in redundant sample ratio. Use 3 random seeds per experiment, with ablation studies isolating the effect of each filtering signal and the adaptive controller.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior methods which use static or heuristic noise filtering based solely on textual similarity and simple statistics, this approach uniquely integrates multi-modal redundancy signals including semantic embeddings and dynamically adapts filtering thresholds based on direct model feedback during scaling phases, enabling sustained or improved scaling laws despite noisy web-scale corpora. Prior work has not combined these elements into an end-to-end adaptive framework guided by reinforcement learning to optimize scaling efficiency and downstream zero-shot generalization.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a principled scaling law framework that explicitly models the interplay between synthetic data quality, noise levels, and model capacity for low-resource language LLMs, optimizing data augmentation weighting schedules to maximize downstream task accuracy under data scarcity.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for large language models largely assume clean, high-quality data and do not account for the influence of synthetic or noisy augmented data typical in low-resource language scenarios. This oversight limits the effectiveness of scaling parameters when training on augmented datasets, as it fails to model how noisy synthetic distributions impact generalization and convergence. The open problem is to establish predictive scaling laws that integrate augmented data characteristics—such as noise distribution, domain shift, and linguistic divergence—so as to guide efficient model scaling and augmentation strategies for low-resource languages.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel analytical and empirical framework that extends classical scaling laws by incorporating an augmentation-aware term quantifying synthetic data quality and noise distribution. This term modulates the effective data distribution seen by the model and influences optimal parameter scaling and compute allocation. We design a curriculum learning scheme where the weighting of augmented versus real low-resource data is dynamically optimized during pretraining to minimize generalization error. Our method jointly tunes model size, augmentation ratio, and weighting schedule using a bi-level optimization approach guided by validation loss on downstream tasks.

EXPERIMENT:  
We validate on multilingual low-resource benchmarks XTREME-R (including Wolof and Quechua), MasakhaNER (African languages), and FLoRes-200 (translation tasks). Baselines include GPT-3 175B, mBERT-base, and XLM-R-large. Metrics are task accuracy improvements (>10% absolute over no-augmentation scaling baselines), F1 score (>8% boost on named entity recognition), and perplexity reductions (>15% relative) on low-resource datasets. Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 clusters with 3 seeds each, including ablations on augmentation noise levels, model sizes (1B to 20B parameters), and augmentation weighting schedules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that ignores the distributional impact of synthetic augmented data on scaling laws or treats augmentation as a fixed preprocessing step, our method explicitly integrates augmentation quality and noise characteristics into scaling law formulations. This enables predictive and optimized scaling strategies tailored for low-resource languages, surpassing approaches like “Data Augmentation-Driven Scaling” by providing a theoretically grounded and empirically validated model of augmentation-data-model interactions.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Energy-aware, event-triggered causal representation learning with compressed causal embedding synchronization for robust inference on intermittently connected edge healthcare IoT devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery and representation learning methods assume continuous network connectivity and frequent model updates, which lead to excessive energy consumption and are impractical for edge devices in healthcare IoT scenarios with intermittent connectivity and sparse labeled data. Existing approaches do not jointly optimize for causal representation fidelity, energy efficiency, and network-aware synchronization delays, limiting deployment in real-world resource-constrained environments.

APPROACH:  
We propose an event-triggered causal representation learning framework that performs model updates only upon detection of significant causal structure changes or evidence accumulation, combined with an energy-aware scheduling mechanism that adapts update frequency based on residual battery and network availability. To minimize communication overhead during synchronization across edge devices and central servers, we develop a novel compressed causal embedding synchronization protocol leveraging sparse coding and quantization techniques tailored for causal factor embeddings. This method preserves causal inference quality despite sparse labels and network dropouts by robustly integrating multi-device causal evidence asynchronously.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on PAMAP2 Physical Activity, MIMIC-III clinical time series, and the UCI Gas Sensor Array Drift dataset to benchmark healthcare and sensor IoT scenarios. Compare against KiNCD, S-FCI, and the baseline continuous causal representation learning approach from the winning tournament entry. Metrics include energy consumption reduction (target ≥30%), causal inference F1-score (target ≥0.8), and synchronization latency under network dropout (target <5 minutes). Experiments run on embedded edge hardware simulators with 5 different random seeds and ablation studies on event-trigger thresholds and embedding compression ratios.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and S-FCI, which focus on causal structure accuracy without resource constraints, and the tournament winner’s continuous update and synchronization strategy, our method innovates by integrating event-triggered update policies with compressed, asynchronous embedding communication specifically optimized for edge healthcare IoT. This achieves substantial energy savings and synchronization resilience without sacrificing causal inference accuracy under realistic intermittent connectivity and sparse labeling conditions.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Meta-learn a hierarchical Bayesian causal discovery framework that integrates patient population-level nonlinear causal priors with uncertainty-aware personalization to enable accurate few-shot causal effect estimation from sparse longitudinal clinical observational data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current methods for causal discovery in personalized medicine fail to effectively combine general population-level causal knowledge with individual patient-specific adaptations when sample sizes per patient are extremely limited, leading to inaccurate or biased causal inferences. Existing nonlinear causal discovery methods like KiNCD and multi-environment causal learning approaches do not support rapid Bayesian hierarchical adaptation with uncertainty quantification for few-shot personalized inference, especially under nonlinear and high-dimensional observational clinical data with latent confounders.

APPROACH:  
We propose a meta-learning framework that pretrains a nonlinear causal discovery model using Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN)-based representations on large-scale longitudinal observational datasets (e.g., eICU and MIMIC-IV). This model captures population-level causal priors and complex nonlinear mechanisms. During patient-level adaptation, we employ a hierarchical Bayesian model with variational inference to personalize causal graphs and effect size estimates from few longitudinal observations per patient, explicitly modeling patient-specific confounders and uncertainty. The framework includes uncertainty calibration via Bayesian posterior predictive checks and uses a novel Bayesian personalization loss that balances population prior adherence with patient data likelihood.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on longitudinal clinical datasets eICU, MIMIC-IV, and synthetic few-shot patient datasets generated by simulating causal mechanisms with known ground truth. Baselines include KiNCD, S-FCI, and the winning Meta-Learned Causal Discovery with Bayesian Personalization method. Metrics include causal graph Structural Hamming Distance (SHD), Average Treatment Effect (ATE) estimation error, and uncertainty calibration metrics like Expected Calibration Error (ECE). Target improvements are >10% absolute reduction in SHD and ATE error, and >15% better uncertainty calibration over baselines. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a training budget of ~72 hours, using 5 random seeds and ablation studies on components like nonlinear priors, Bayesian personalization, and uncertainty calibration.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and multi-environment methods which do not perform hierarchical Bayesian adaptation, our approach uniquely integrates nonlinear causal priors with Bayesian meta-learning and uncertainty quantification for few-shot patient personalization. This enables reliable causal discovery and effect estimation in extremely data-scarce clinical settings where prior methods cannot balance generalization and individual-specific confounding with calibrated uncertainty.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a resource- and privacy-efficient contrastive causal representation learning framework that incrementally discovers invariant causal features from streaming, decentralized observational data on Healthcare IoT devices without centralized data aggregation.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery methods fail to operate effectively under the dual constraints of strict resource limitations on edge healthcare IoT devices and stringent privacy restrictions that prevent centralized data pooling. Existing approaches like KiNCD and S-FCI either require centralized datasets or do not support incremental updates on streaming data, while contrastive methods have not been adapted for causal representation learning in decentralized, privacy-preserving healthcare IoT environments. This gap blocks real-time, on-device causal inference essential for proactive and personalized healthcare monitoring.

APPROACH:  
We propose Contrastive Incremental Causal Representation Learning (CICRL), a lightweight framework that performs continual causal feature extraction by leveraging contrastive learning between temporally adjacent and contextually perturbed streaming data batches locally on-device. CICRL combines a scalable kernelized invariant risk minimization loss with a resource-aware neural architecture based on small Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) to model nonlinear causal relations. Privacy is preserved by training exclusively on-device with locally perturbed augmentations, and a decentralized consensus mechanism enables periodic lightweight synchronization of causal representations across devices without sharing raw data. This approach supports incremental updates, handles nonlinearities, and ensures robustness to distributional shifts in streaming healthcare data.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate CICRL on real-world healthcare IoT benchmark datasets: (1) MIMIC-III waveform and vital signs data subset, (2) PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring dataset, and (3) PhysioNet Challenge 2012 data. Baselines include KiNCD, S-FCI, and contrastive causal representation learning (prior centralized methods). Metrics: Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) for causal graph accuracy (target >5% absolute improvement over KiNCD), F1-score for causal edge detection (>3% absolute improvement), and memory/compute efficiency (reduce on-device latency by ≥30% compared to KiNCD). Protocol involves 5 random seeds, ablations on kernel size and augmentation strategies, and evaluation under simulated device memory and compute constraints (edge CPU class hardware, ≤500MB RAM). Experiments will also measure privacy leakage under membership inference attacks.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and S-FCI, CICRL is the first to enable incremental, on-device causal discovery from streaming observational data under strict resource and privacy constraints, without centralized aggregation. Unlike previous contrastive causal methods, it fuses kernelized invariant risk minimization with lightweight KAN architectures optimized for edge deployment and leverages decentralized consensus to preserve privacy while improving causal representation robustness across heterogeneous device environments.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical, memory-adaptive graph neural network (H-MaGNN) framework that dynamically adjusts graph coarsening granularity and sparse attention patterns during causal discovery to enable end-to-end scalable, accurate, and memory-efficient learning of causal graphs with tens of thousands of nodes from purely observational data.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing GNN-based causal discovery methods, including the winning Memory-Constrained Graph Neural Network Search, cannot scale efficiently beyond several thousand nodes due to fixed graph coarsening and sparse attention mechanisms that do not adapt to heterogeneous graph structures or memory availability during training. This limits their applicability to extremely large-scale causal systems such as whole-genome regulatory networks or large social interaction graphs. The open problem is how to design a method that dynamically balances memory consumption and representation fidelity to scale causal structure learning to orders of magnitude larger graphs with nonuniform node connectivity, while maintaining or improving discovery accuracy.

APPROACH:  
We propose H-MaGNN, a hierarchical graph neural network that incorporates a memory-adaptive controller to modulate the level of graph coarsening and sparse attention pattern density at each training iteration based on current GPU/TPU memory usage and node degree heterogeneity. The model learns to perform differentiable graph pooling/unpooling at multiple hierarchical levels, preserving key causal motifs while aggressively compressing less informative subgraphs. Sparse attention is parametrically conditioned on node centrality and edge type features to focus message passing on plausible causal edges under nonlinear dependencies. This enables a continuous trade-off between memory footprint and causal score fidelity within a unified end-to-end differentiable architecture, outperforming fixed-coarsening baselines.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate H-MaGNN on large synthetic causal graphs with 5,000 to 20,000 nodes and real-world biological datasets including the DREAM5 Gene Regulatory Network Challenge data and the TCGA breast cancer multi-omics dataset, as well as the Facebook Large Page-Page Network. Baselines will include Memory-Constrained Graph Neural Network Search, KiNCD, and Orthogonal Structure Search. Metrics include Structural Hamming Distance (SHD, target >5% reduction vs baselines), Area Under Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC, target >3% absolute improvement), and peak GPU memory usage (target ≥30% reduction). Experiments will use 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with mixed precision training, running 5 seeds per configuration and ablation studies on hierarchy depth and adaptive attention parameters.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior fixed-memory, fixed-graph compression approaches (e.g., Memory-Constrained Graph Neural Network Search), H-MaGNN introduces a dynamic, learnable memory-adaptive mechanism that continuously balances graph detail preservation and memory footprint in large-scale causal discovery. It handles extreme scale causal graphs with heterogeneous topology more effectively and with verifiable causal recovery improvements, which prior work cannot achieve under stringent memory constraints.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a privacy-preserving, irregular time-series invariant causal discovery framework that leverages domain-adaptive representation learning and federated invariant mechanism detection to enable robust personalized healthcare monitoring from heterogeneous observational data streams.

BACKGROUND:  
Current invariant causal discovery methods are ineffective for personalized healthcare because they assume regularly sampled, population-level data and do not adequately handle the irregular, asynchronous, and highly individualized physiological data collected under strict privacy constraints. Moreover, existing frameworks cannot jointly manage nonlinear causal mechanisms while ensuring privacy-preserving learning across distributed, sensitive patient data streams. This creates a critical gap in reliable causal inference for personalized interventions in real-world healthcare monitoring.

APPROACH:  
We propose a federated causal discovery framework that integrates (1) domain-adaptive neural representations to homogenize irregularly sampled, multimodal physiological signals across patients; (2) a novel invariant mechanism detection algorithm employing continuous optimization over the discovered latent representations to identify stable causal relations invariant across individuals and temporal environments; and (3) privacy guarantees via differential privacy and secure multi-party computation protocols enabling decentralized model training without data sharing. The approach dynamically adapts to individualized sampling patterns while enforcing invariance constraints to isolate genuine causal mechanisms from spurious correlations under privacy constraints.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark datasets: (1) MIMIC-III Waveform Database for ICU physiological signals, (2) PhysioNet Challenge 2012 dataset, and (3) UK Biobank wearable sensor data. Compare against baselines KiNCD (2025), S-FCI (2023), and Orthogonal Structure Search (2019). Metrics: Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) for causal graph accuracy (targeting >5% absolute improvement), Invariant Mechanism Detection Accuracy (>10% improvement), and privacy budget (ε <1 under differential privacy). Conduct experiments on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds, including ablations on privacy vs. accuracy trade-offs and irregular sampling robustness.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and S-FCI, our method explicitly handles irregular, personalized sampling and enforces invariance under decentralized, privacy-preserving federated training, which prior work cannot achieve. It uniquely integrates domain-adaptive neural embeddings with invariant mechanism detection in a privacy-sensitive federated setting, enabling causal discovery from real-world, heterogeneous healthcare data streams.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop bio-inspired, ultra-low power analog synaptic devices with adaptive noise-filtering capabilities tailored for wearable biomedical sensors, achieving stable synaptic plasticity and energy efficiency under physiological conditions that degrade existing neuromorphic components.

BACKGROUND:  
Wearable biomedical sensors require synaptic devices that operate reliably with minimal energy in dynamic, noisy biological environments. Current neuromorphic synaptic devices lack adequate plasticity stability and energy efficiency when exposed to physiological variability such as temperature fluctuations, motion artifacts, and biochemical noise, limiting their practical deployment in long-duration wearable applications. The open problem is to design synaptic devices that maintain stable learning and memory functions with ultra-low power consumption specifically optimized for the complex, noisy conditions of wearable biomedical sensing.

APPROACH:  
We propose engineering bio-inspired synaptic devices integrating an adaptive noise-filtering mechanism based on ionic-electronic hybrid conductance modulation combined with a biomimetic feedback loop that dynamically stabilizes synaptic weights. This involves fabricating electrolyte-gated transistor arrays with embedded bio-mimetic ion channels that emulate homeostatic plasticity, enabling self-correction in response to physiological noise. The resulting devices will exhibit stable long-term potentiation/depression under varying environmental and biological conditions, while reducing energy dissipation by at least 40% relative to current neuromorphic synapses in wearable contexts.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate device performance on neuromorphic versions of the PhysioNet EEG Motor Movement/Imagery Dataset, MIMIC-III physiological signals dataset, and the PPG-DaLiA dataset for photoplethysmography-based activity recognition. Baselines will include the 2TFGM synaptic device (2021), the vertical dual-gate neurotransistor (2023), and the ferroelectric DropConnect synaptic transistor (2025). Metrics include synaptic plasticity stability (measured via weight update linearity and retention time), energy per synaptic event (target < 30 fJ), classification accuracy improvements >3% absolute on biomedical signal recognition tasks, and noise robustness quantified by signal-to-noise ratio improvements >20% under simulated physiological perturbations. Experiments will run with a training budget of up to 1,000 device cycles per condition, repeated across 5 seeds, including ablations removing the adaptive feedback to isolate its contribution.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that optimize energy or precision in clean or controlled environments, this approach uniquely integrates adaptive biomimetic noise-filtering at the device level, enabling robust synaptic plasticity and energy efficiency specifically in the dynamic, noisy physiological conditions of wearable sensors—a capability absent from existing 2D material or electrolyte-gated synapses.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a workload-predictive, hierarchical adaptive power gating framework integrated with synapse-level activity sparsity detection for neuromorphic processors, enabling fine-grained, multi-scale dynamic power gating that reduces energy consumption by over 50% during intermittent IoT workloads while maintaining ≤1.5% accuracy loss.

BACKGROUND:  
Intermittently active IoT devices running neuromorphic processors suffer from excessive static and idle power consumption due to coarse or static power gating approaches that cannot adapt well to sparse and irregular sensor events. Existing adaptive power gating methods, including workload prediction tied to firing patterns, only gate at coarse module levels and do not exploit synaptic activity sparsity or hierarchy within neuromorphic cores, limiting potential energy savings and responsiveness. A method is needed to tightly combine multi-level workload prediction with synapse-level gating to minimize idle power without sacrificing accuracy or latency.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical adaptive power gating scheme that operates at multiple levels: the chip, core, and down to synapse-array subcircuits. Using LSTM-based workload prediction models trained on temporal event patterns from sensor inputs, the framework dynamically gates inactive cores and synapse blocks before idle periods. Additionally, real-time synaptic activity sparsity detection triggers fine-grained gating of unused synapse arrays during event-driven bursts. This dual predictive and reactive gating is co-designed with neuromorphic firing pattern monitors to ensure gating does not degrade responsiveness or classification accuracy. The method dynamically balances gating aggressiveness per workload phase and device-level power-performance targets.

EXPERIMENT:  
Validation will use the N-CARS neuromorphic dataset and the DVS Gesture dataset for event-based sensor workloads, alongside the widely used MNIST-DVS spiking dataset to test generality. Baselines include the always-on neuromorphic processor, the original Adaptive Power Gating approach (from the tournament winner), and a static fine-grained power gating scheme. Metrics are energy reduction (target >50% lower average power during idle compared to always-on), classification accuracy (≤1.5% absolute drop from always-on), and latency overhead (<5% increase in event classification latency). Experiments will run on a neuromorphic hardware simulator supporting hierarchical gating with three independent seeds per configuration and ablations on gating granularity and prediction model complexity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which gates power at coarse module levels based on firing patterns alone, this proposal tightly couples multi-scale workload prediction with real-time synaptic activity sparsity detection to achieve unprecedented fine-grained, hierarchical power gating. Existing optoelectronic synapses or synaptic transistor innovations focus on device-level efficiency but do not address dynamic idle power management. This approach enables dynamic, predictive gating at synapse-array granularity integrated into neuromorphic cores, substantially surpassing static or single-level adaptive gating schemes in energy savings without sacrificing accuracy or responsiveness.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Develop an energy-aware, adaptive spiking plasticity framework optimized for wearable neuromorphic health monitoring that dynamically balances accuracy, latency, and power under intermittent and noisy physiological data conditions.

BACKGROUND: Wearable health monitoring devices face extreme constraints on power, space, and processing latency while continuously interpreting noisy and irregular physiological signals (e.g., ECG, PPG). Existing neuromorphic plasticity models and hardware advances fail to jointly optimize spiking synaptic adaptation for minimal energy consumption and robustness to intermittent inputs typical of wearable environments. This bottleneck prevents reliable, always-on, low-latency health event detection at ultra-low power budgets essential for practical deployment.

APPROACH: We propose a novel neuromorphic plasticity model that dynamically modulates spiking synaptic weight updates based on real-time signal quality and energy availability, implemented on a low-power event-driven neuromorphic processor prototype. Key components include an energy-aware spiking Hebbian learning rule with noise-adaptive thresholds and plasticity gating, combined with an intermittent input-aware temporal encoding scheme that preserves critical physiological features. The approach integrates software-hardware co-design, leveraging a compact plasticity module optimized for ultra-low energy on-chip synaptic updates.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation will use the PhysioNet MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database, WESAD (Wearable Stress and Affect Detection), and the MIMIC-III Waveform Database, benchmarking against the spiking plasticity baseline from "Convolutional networks for fast, energy-efficient neuromorphic computing" (2016), the ferroelectric synaptic transistor DropConnect model (2025), and the 2TFGM synapse device implementation (2021). Metrics will include >3% absolute improvement in F1-score for arrhythmia/stress event detection, >25% latency reduction relative to baseline spiking models, and >30% decrease in synaptic update energy per event. Experiments will run on neuromorphic hardware emulators with a fixed compute budget of 1000 GPU-hours for training, with at least five random seeds, including ablation studies on noise adaptation and energy gating components.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior neuromorphic plasticity schemes and hardware synapses that do not explicitly address the joint challenges of intermittent, noisy wearable biosignals and energy-constrained adaptive learning, this work introduces a dynamic, energy-aware plasticity mechanism optimized for wearable sensing environments, enabling robust, real-time physiological event detection with substantially reduced power consumption and latency.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a noise- and power-interruption-resilient analog in-memory neuromorphic computing architecture that dynamically adapts operational parameters to maintain ultra-low power consumption and high computational accuracy on edge IoT devices under fluctuating environmental and power conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current analog in-memory computing neuromorphic approaches for edge IoT devices fail to reliably maintain computational precision in the presence of power variability and environmental noise, which results in degraded performance and limits their deployment in real-world autonomous edge applications. Prior devices and architectures either sacrifice robustness or energy efficiency, lacking dynamic mechanisms to counteract intermittent power and environmental disturbances without incurring significant overhead. Addressing this bottleneck is critical for ensuring sustained, energy-efficient neuromorphic operation in resource-constrained, variable environments.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid adaptive analog in-memory computing architecture incorporating real-time calibration circuits and environment-aware power gating, combined with noise-adaptive signal encoding schemes to maintain synaptic weight fidelity and neuron activation stability despite power interruptions and noise fluctuations. Key components include low-overhead in-situ noise sensing with feedback control to dynamically adjust device biasing and update rates, and a redundancy-aware analog weight storage employing error-correcting codes optimized for analog domain variability. The system integrates compact analog calibration modules embedded within a memristive crossbar array fabricated using energy-efficient 2D-material-based synaptic devices to minimize area and power costs.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate the architecture on neuromorphic inference tasks using the **Neuromorphic MNIST (N-MNIST)**, **DVS128 Gesture Dataset**, and **Google Speech Commands v2** dataset to capture vision and audio edge use cases. Baselines include **ResNet-18 (quantized for edge)**, **DVSNet**, and **FERROSynapticNet** (ferroelectric synapse-based model). Metrics to measure are: classification accuracy (target >2% absolute improvement under noisy/power-variable conditions vs baselines), energy consumption per inference (target <10 fJ per synaptic operation, achieving ≥30% reduction), and system uptime under intermittent power scenarios (target >50% extension vs baseline without adaptive calibration). The experimental protocol involves training and testing under simulated environmental noise and power cycling using a 50 mW edge neuromorphic hardware platform prototype, with at least 5 random seeds per configuration, alongside ablation studies isolating the effect of adaptive calibration, power gating, and analog ECC.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior neuromorphic analog in-memory approaches that either ignore power variability and environmental noise or handle them with static, offline calibration, our method provides an integrated, real-time adaptive control mechanism that dynamically preserves computational accuracy and energy efficiency simultaneously. This bridges the gap between ultra-low power analog computing and robust operation under real-world edge IoT conditions, which prior works such as ferroelectric synaptic transistors or 2D material synapses cannot achieve due to lack of embedded adaptive resilience features.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an adaptive, low-overhead in-memory error correction framework for analog memristive synapses that dynamically optimizes error resilience based on real-time synaptic noise profiling and workload characteristics, achieving robust edge neuromorphic inference with sub-5% energy overhead and minimal latency impact.

BACKGROUND:  
Current in-memory error correction schemes for analog memristive synapses in neuromorphic computing, such as those designed for drones, reduce weight perturbations but incur non-negligible energy and latency costs that limit scalability and adaptability to varying operating conditions. Existing methods do not dynamically adjust error correction intensity based on synapse noise variability or task-specific tolerance, resulting in either unnecessary overhead or degraded inference reliability under fluctuating hardware and environmental conditions.

APPROACH:  
We propose an integrated in-memory error correction architecture that continuously profiles analog synaptic noise and activation sparsity patterns during operation, using lightweight on-chip circuitry to modulate error correction code (ECC) strength adaptively. This involves a multi-tier ECC scheme combining low-complexity codes (e.g., lightweight BCH or Hamming variants) with analog-domain noise-aware calibration, selectively applied only when noise surpasses a learned threshold or critical inference tasks demand higher reliability. The system schedules ECC activation per memory subarray using learned policies to optimize energy versus accuracy trade-offs dynamically, implemented on a memristive crossbar array with co-designed peripheral circuits.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark neuromorphic and edge AI datasets UAV123 (object tracking), CIFAR-10 (image classification), and N-MNIST (neuromorphic event-based vision), comparing against baselines Low-Overhead ECC for Analog Memristive Synapses (the tournament winner), standard Hamming ECC, and no ECC. Metrics include inference accuracy (>95% target on UAV123), energy overhead (target <5%, improving over the prior <10%), latency increase (<5% overhead), and ECC activation frequency. Experiments run on FPGA-accelerated memristive crossbar emulators with at least 5 random seeds; ablations analyze ECC granularity, threshold sensitivity, and workload adaptivity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior fixed-scheme ECC methods which apply uniform error correction incurring static overhead, our approach uniquely combines real-time noise profiling with adaptive ECC strength modulation directly in analog memristive arrays, enabling significant energy savings and latency reduction while preserving or improving inference accuracy under variable noise and workload conditions. This dynamic adaptability is absent in both the winning method and other state-of-the-art neuromorphic synapse error correction or device-level innovations.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Develop a physics-informed, transformer-based generative model integrated with differentiable molecular simulations for ultra-high-throughput virtual screening that ensures physically valid, drug-like molecule generation while reducing simulation cost by an order of magnitude.

BACKGROUND: Current generative AI models for drug discovery either generate molecules without explicit physical constraints—leading to chemically invalid or non-druggable candidates—or rely on computationally expensive physics-based simulations that limit screening throughput to thousands rather than millions of molecules. This bottleneck restricts real-time applicability in early-stage drug discovery, where both scale and accuracy are critical for identifying promising leads efficiently.

APPROACH: We propose a novel architecture combining a physics-informed transformer generative model with embedded differentiable molecular physics modules (e.g., differentiable force fields and quantum-inspired energy predictors) to guide molecule generation. This model learns to propose candidate molecules that inherently satisfy physical and chemical constraints, leveraging a multi-task loss that integrates energy-based validity, synthetic accessibility, and drug-likeness metrics. To accelerate evaluation, we incorporate a learned surrogate model trained on high-fidelity QM/MM simulation data, enabling rapid on-the-fly energy estimation and filtering during generation. The entire pipeline operates end-to-end on GPU clusters, facilitating generation and screening of millions of candidates per hour.

EXPERIMENT: We will benchmark on the ZINC15, ChEMBL, and QM9 datasets for molecular generation and property prediction. Baselines include MolGPT, GFlowNet, and Physics-Guided Graph VAE. Evaluation metrics are validity (>98%), uniqueness (>90%), novelty (>85%), docking score correlation (>0.7 Pearson to rigorously computed docking), and throughput (molecules generated and screened per hour) aiming for at least 10x speedup over Physics-Guided Graph VAE with comparable or better physical validity. Experiments will use 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for training over 5 seeds, including ablations of physics loss terms and surrogate accuracy.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that either sacrifices physics for speed or relies on costly simulations, our approach tightly integrates differentiable physics constraints within a transformer generative model and employs a learned surrogate for rapid evaluation, achieving scalable, high-fidelity generation and screening simultaneously—enabling real-time virtual screening at previously unattainable scales.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate Bayesian reward uncertainty modeling into multi-agent reinforcement learning combined with contrastive molecular representation learning to robustly optimize diverse drug properties and enable efficient exploration in noisy, sparse assay environments for multi-objective drug lead optimization.

BACKGROUND:  
Current multi-objective reinforcement learning (RL) methods for drug lead optimization struggle with unstable policy updates and inefficient exploration due to noisy, sparse, and uncertain reward signals from in vitro assay feedback, limiting sample efficiency and property balancing across multiple objectives. Existing approaches, such as single-agent Bayesian reward uncertainty modeling, improve robustness but fail to fully capture the complex trade-offs and exploration-exploitation dynamics inherent in multi-objective molecular design, especially under high assay noise and limited data.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel framework that combines Bayesian modeling of reward uncertainty within a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) paradigm, where multiple agents specialize in optimizing different drug properties. Agents share a contrastively learned molecular embedding space that captures nuanced chemical features and promotes better generalization. Bayesian uncertainty guides not only intra-agent policy updates but also inter-agent exploration coordination, enabling adaptive balancing of conflicting objectives despite noisy and sparse rewards. Policies are updated via stable off-policy algorithms with uncertainty-aware exploration bonuses. The framework incorporates molecular graph transformers fine-tuned with contrastive learning on large chemical datasets to produce robust representations informing the reward model and policy networks.

EXPERIMENT:
