We will evaluate on three benchmark datasets: GuacaMol (goal-directed benchmarks), MOSES (molecular optimization tasks), and ChEMBL (bioactivity data for multi-objective property prediction). Baselines include standard RL methods with fixed rewards (REINVENT, MolDQN), Bayesian reward uncertainty RL (Multi-Objective RL with Bayesian Reward Uncertainty), and MARL without uncertainty (QMIX, MADDPG). Quantitative metrics will include:  
- Property improvement scores (e.g., QED, LogP, and synthetic accessibility) with >10% absolute increase over baselines,  
- Sample efficiency measured by number of molecules generated to reach target scores reduced by ≥30%,  
- Reproducibility measured by variance of property scores across 5 independent runs reduced by ≥25%,  
- Pareto front coverage and hypervolume improvement by ≥15% versus MARL baselines.  

We will train on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for up to 100k environment interactions per run, conducting 5 runs per configuration and performing ablation studies isolating Bayesian uncertainty modeling, MARL, and contrastive molecular embeddings.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that applies Bayesian reward uncertainty modeling only within single-agent RL, our approach introduces multi-agent collaboration with uncertainty-aware exploration coordination, enabling more stable and efficient multi-objective optimization under noisy assay signals. The integration of contrastive molecular representation learning with Bayesian uncertainty-guided MARL is novel and addresses limitations in exploration and policy stability that prior single-agent or purely generative models cannot overcome.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a real-time, interpretable reinforcement learning framework for adaptive molecular design that dynamically optimizes patient-specific drug candidates during clinical treatment, incorporating safety constraints and rapid convergence for deployment in regulated healthcare environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI models for drug discovery generate candidate molecules offline without the capability to adapt designs in real-time based on evolving patient data or therapeutic responses. This limits their utility in clinical settings where drug optimization requires rapid, transparent, and safe iterations tailored to individual patients under strict regulatory oversight. The open problem is how to design an AI system that can integrate real-time patient feedback to adjust molecular structures dynamically while ensuring interpretability and compliance with clinical safety standards.

APPROACH:  
We propose a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that leverages patient-specific multi-modal data streams (e.g., genomic profiles, biomarker levels, and treatment response metrics) to iteratively generate and refine molecular candidates during clinical treatment. Key components include: (1) a molecular generator conditioned on patient state embeddings using Transformer-based architectures, (2) a transparent reward function incorporating efficacy, toxicity, and regulatory safety constraints, and (3) a real-time adaptive policy trained with model-based RL for faster convergence. The system will employ uncertainty quantification methods (e.g., Bayesian neural networks) to ensure decision interpretability and safety assurance.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark datasets including ChEMBL (drug bioactivity), MoleculeNet’s Tox21 (toxicity data), and PK/PD datasets such as the DrugBank clinical trial subset. Baselines include MolDQN, GraphAF, and REINVENT 2.0. Metrics measured will be drug-likeness (QED score), predicted toxicity reduction (>15% improvement over baselines), therapeutic target affinity (binding affinity improvement >10%), and convergence speed (reduce training episodes by ≥30%). Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds, including ablations on reward components and interpretability modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior generative AI work which operates offline or focuses on static datasets, this method enables real-time, patient-adaptive molecular design with interpretable decisions and explicit safety constraints, addressing the unique demands of clinical environments. It integrates multi-modal patient data into molecular generation and guarantees faster convergence and regulatory compliance not achieved by existing approaches.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Develop a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for on-device molecular design that enables real-time, multi-objective optimization of diagnostic probes on resource-constrained edge devices using model compression and efficient reward approximation.

BACKGROUND: Current RL-based molecular design systems require high memory and computational resources, preventing their use on portable diagnostic devices where real-time molecular exploration and multi-objective optimization (e.g., affinity, specificity, synthesis feasibility) are critical. Existing generative AI models also lack adaptation for edge deployment, limiting decentralized point-of-care molecular innovation.

APPROACH: We propose a lightweight molecular generation RL agent that integrates: (1) a distilled transformer model with quantized weights for efficient molecular structure generation, (2) a novel reward approximation module leveraging precomputed surrogate models for rapid multi-objective scoring, and (3) an on-device incremental learning mechanism to personalize probe design based on local data. The system will run entirely on ARM-based edge hardware with minimal memory (<500MB) and energy (<2W) footprints.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark datasets including MOSES, ZINC-250K, and ChEMBL for molecular generation quality and diversity; compare against baselines such as MolDQN, REINVENT, and GraphAF. Metrics include >15% improvement in multi-objective optimization scores (e.g., QED, synthetic accessibility, target affinity proxy), inference latency <100ms per molecule, and memory usage reduction >50%. Experiments will use 3 random seeds, 48-hour training budget on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX, and include ablation studies on reward approximation fidelity and model compression level.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior generative AI or RL models limited to cloud or high-resource environments, this method enables fully on-device molecular design with real-time multi-objective optimization, overcoming memory and compute bottlenecks through model distillation, quantization, and surrogate reward modeling. This capability directly addresses deployment constraints in portable diagnostics, which existing frameworks cannot support.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Develop a physics- and reaction-informed generative AI model that simultaneously optimizes molecular design for drug-likeness, target bioactivity, and experimentally validated synthetic accessibility by integrating retrosynthetic route prediction and lab-scale reaction constraints into the molecule generation process.

BACKGROUND: Current generative AI models for drug discovery often produce molecules that are theoretically promising but synthetically infeasible, limiting their translational potential. The open problem is the lack of integration between molecular generation and practical synthetic route feasibility, leading to a disconnect between in silico design and experimental realization. Overcoming this bottleneck requires embedding real-world synthetic constraints and reaction pathway predictions directly into generative frameworks.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-objective generative architecture combining a graph-based molecular generator with embedded synthetic accessibility scoring derived from state-of-the-art retrosynthesis prediction tools (e.g., ASKCOS and IBM RXN). The model uses reinforcement learning to optimize not only drug-like properties (e.g., QED, binding affinity proxies) but also predicted synthetic routes constrained by lab-scale reaction conditions such as reagent availability, reaction yields, and step count. A differentiable synthetic feasibility module guides generation toward molecules with high-confidence, low-complexity synthetic pathways, balancing physicochemical stability and practical synthesis.

EXPERIMENT: Train and evaluate on benchmark datasets including ZINC15, ChEMBL, and USPTO reaction dataset to benchmark synthetic route prediction. Baselines for comparison include the original GraphVAE, MolGPT, and the physics-informed generative model from the tournament winner paper. Metrics include: (1) Synthetic Accessibility Score (SAScore) with target improvement >15% over baselines; (2) Retrosynthetic route confidence and step count from ASKCOS with >20% increase in experimentally validated routes; (3) Drug-likeness measured by QED with stable performance; (4) Binding affinity proxy improvements on benchmark targets (e.g., EGFR inhibitors dataset) with >5% absolute improvement in docking score. Experimental protocol includes training on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs over 10 runs with different random seeds, accompanied by ablation studies removing synthetic constraints to quantify their impact.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior models that treat synthetic accessibility as a post hoc filter or approximate heuristic, this approach tightly integrates chemical reaction knowledge and retrosynthetic feasibility within the generative loop, enabling the design of molecules that are simultaneously optimized for drug activity and real-world synthetic accessibility under laboratory constraints. This closes the critical gap between molecular design and experimental synthesis, a capability absent in existing generation frameworks.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
A federated learning framework that dynamically adapts client-specific model architectures and communication frequencies through hierarchical meta-learning and multi-granular shift detection to efficiently handle extreme client heterogeneity and rapidly evolving non-IID data distributions in resource-constrained wearable environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing federated learning methods inadequately address the combined challenges of highly heterogeneous clients (varying computation, communication capabilities, and data distributions) and rapidly non-stationary, non-IID data streams common in wearable health applications. Current approaches such as adaptive meta-updates reduce communication overhead but treat client models and update triggers largely uniformly, leading to suboptimal personalization, excessive communication, or slow adaptation in highly dynamic, resource-constrained settings.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical meta-learning architecture that simultaneously optimizes (1) personalized, lightweight model architectures per client via neural architecture search constrained by client resource profiles, and (2) adaptive communication policies driven by a multi-granular online shift detection module that distinguishes between transient noise, local distribution drifts, and global non-stationarities. This mechanism selectively triggers meta-updates at both client and cluster levels, dynamically balancing communication cost and model freshness. Clients are clustered dynamically based on evolving data similarity to further optimize communication and knowledge sharing. The framework integrates resource-aware pruning and quantization to ensure feasibility on wearables.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on PAMAP2 and PPG-DaLiA (wearable sensor datasets with multimodal, non-stationary biometric signals) plus additional real-world federated health datasets such as MobiAct and WESAD. Baselines include (1) Adaptive Communication-Efficient Meta-Updates (the tournament winner), (2) FedGKD (Local-Global Knowledge Distillation), and (3) HeteroSFL (Split Federated Learning for heterogeneous clients). Metrics: (a) personalization accuracy improvement >3% absolute over baselines, (b) communication reduction >40% vs Adaptive Meta-Updates, and (c) energy consumption on client devices reduced by >25%. Training runs will be conducted on edge simulation clusters with 10 heterogeneous clients per run, 5 seeds each, including ablations on model architecture adaptation, cluster granularity, and shift detection sensitivity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that applies uniform models or uniform meta-update triggers across heterogeneous clients, our method uniquely combines personalized architecture search with hierarchical, multi-level shift detection and adaptive communication scheduling. This enables efficient learning in extreme heterogeneity and fast-changing non-IID wearable data streams, which existing meta-update or split learning methods cannot simultaneously achieve without excessive overhead or accuracy loss.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
A meta-learning framework for federated learning in IoT sensor networks that jointly models sparse, noisy, and heterogeneous non-IID data distributions with resource-constrained edge personalization through adaptive model splitting and noise-robust local fine-tuning.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning in IoT sensor networks faces severe challenges due to highly heterogeneous, sparse, and noisy data that is non-IID across devices with diverse sensing modalities and communication capabilities. Existing meta-learning methods either assume richer local data or fail to handle both noisy/sparse inputs and constrained edge compute, resulting in poor personalization and inefficient communication under realistic IoT constraints. The open problem is enabling rapid, robust local personalization on heterogeneous IoT clients without sacrificing energy efficiency or model accuracy amidst non-IID data and device heterogeneity.

APPROACH:  
We propose MetaSplitFL, a meta-learning based federated learning framework that strategically partitions the model between edge clients and servers based on client compute and communication budgets, enabling adaptive local fine-tuning of lightweight personalized heads robust to sparse and noisy inputs. Our method integrates: (1) noise-aware meta-initialization via robust loss functions and data imputation embeddings to handle corrupted sensor data; (2) adaptive model splitting guided by client profiling to optimize the compute-communication tradeoff per client; and (3) a specialized meta-optimization loop that encourages fast adaptation on limited, heterogeneous sensor data distributions, explicitly modeling client-level non-IIDness through task-conditioned adapters. This holistic design ensures efficient, accurate personalization that current methods cannot achieve in severely resource-limited, noisy IoT environments.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on 1) Intel Lab Data (real-world multi-sensor IoT dataset with environmental measurements), 2) PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring (heterogeneous wearable sensor data), and 3) NASA Turbofan Engine Degradation Simulation Dataset (noisy sensor readings from heterogeneous machines). Baselines include: FedGKD (2021), HeteroSFL (2025), and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation (2025). Metrics are: >3% absolute improvement in top-1 accuracy on personalization tasks, 25% reduction in communication overhead vs. HeteroSFL, and latency reduction of >30% on edge devices under fixed energy budgets. Five random seeds with ablation on noise robustness and model splitting components will validate results.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that treats heterogeneity, noise, or sparsity in isolation or assumes richer data and uniform client resources, MetaSplitFL uniquely combines noise-robust meta-learning with dynamic model splitting tailored to heterogeneous, resource-constrained IoT clients, enabling rapid personalization on extremely sparse, corrupted, non-IID sensor data—a capability neither HeteroSFL nor existing meta-learning FL methods provide.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a meta-learned hierarchical client clustering and adaptive aggregation framework that dynamically groups heterogeneous clients by data distribution similarity and jointly optimizes aggregation weights within and across clusters to robustly handle extreme non-IID data heterogeneity at scale.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning (FL) with highly heterogeneous and non-IID client data distributions suffers from biased global models and poor performance on minority or underrepresented client groups. Existing adaptive weighting aggregation methods, including meta-learned importance scores, treat clients independently and fail to exploit latent structure among client data heterogeneity, resulting in limited robustness and scalability at large client diversity. There is a critical need to explicitly model and leverage inter-client relationships and cluster-level heterogeneity for improved global model fairness and convergence.

APPROACH:  
We propose a two-level approach: (1) a meta-learned hierarchical clustering method that dynamically groups clients based on learned representations of their local data distribution and model updates, enabling scalable identification of client subpopulations with similar data characteristics; (2) an adaptive aggregation scheme that learns separate aggregation weight functions within each cluster and a cluster-level weighting mechanism to balance contributions between clusters. This joint meta-learning framework is trained end-to-end during federated rounds, enabling the aggregation weights to adapt to both intra- and inter-cluster heterogeneity simultaneously. Additionally, we incorporate cluster-specific knowledge distillation modules to mitigate client drift and promote knowledge sharing within clusters.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on at least three heterogeneous federated benchmarks: Stack Overflow (next-word prediction), Shakespeare (character-level language modeling), and CIFAR-10 with artificially induced non-IID partitions simulating label and feature skew. Baselines include Adaptive Weighting Aggregation (the tournament winner), FedGKD (Local-Global Knowledge Distillation), and HeteroSFL. Metrics will measure convergence speed (number of rounds to reach target accuracy), overall global model accuracy, and minority client group accuracy, targeting a ≥10% absolute accuracy gain on minority groups and ≥5% faster convergence vs Adaptive Weighting Aggregation. Experiments will run on a cluster with GPUs, using 5 random seeds, and include ablations isolating the clustering and multi-level weighting components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike the tournament winner and prior methods that treat clients independently or rely on fixed heuristics, our method explicitly models hierarchical client relationships through meta-learned clustering and jointly adapts aggregation weights at both cluster and client levels. This structured approach enables scalable handling of extreme heterogeneity and minority client robustness beyond what is achievable by flat weighting schemes or distillation alone.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Latency-Aware Multi-Modal Meta-Federated Learning with Dynamic Client Profiling for Real-Time Personalization on Heterogeneous and Non-IID Edge Devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing meta-learning FL approaches for real-time edge personalization do not adequately address the compounded challenges of extreme client heterogeneity—both in computational capacity and data modality—and non-IID data distributions, particularly under strict latency constraints. Current methods either ignore multi-modal data diversity or assume homogeneous client profiles, resulting in slow adaptation and suboptimal accuracy on heterogeneous edge devices with varying latency and compute budgets.

APPROACH:  
We propose a meta-federated learning framework that dynamically profiles client heterogeneity along three axes: computational capability, network latency, and data modality distribution. The global meta-model initialization is optimized with a latency-constrained multi-objective objective that simultaneously minimizes adaptation time and maximizes accuracy across diverse client profiles. To handle multi-modal non-IID data, we incorporate a modality-aware adaptive model splitting technique that dynamically partitions the model between client and server based on client capability and data modality, reducing client computation and communication overhead. Fast fine-tuning is accelerated via learned latency-aware parameter subsets prioritized during local adaptation, supported by a latency-budgeted regularization term in meta-training. This design enables real-time personalization with guaranteed latency targets without sacrificing accuracy on heterogeneous and multi-modal non-IID datasets.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on Speech Commands (audio), Google Landmark V2 (image), and the Federated Extended MNIST (FEMNIST) datasets to represent heterogeneous multi-modal and non-IID settings. Baselines include Latency-Constrained Meta-Learning (from the tournament winner), HeteroSFL, FedGKD, and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation. Metrics measured are adaptation latency (target <100 ms on edge hardware), accuracy (target >2% absolute improvement over baselines), and energy consumption (target >20% reduction vs Latency-Constrained Meta-Learning). Experiments run on NVIDIA Jetson edge devices and simulated 5G network conditions, with five seeds per setup and ablations on model splitting granularity and latency regularization strength.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either ignores multi-modal heterogeneity or assumes fixed client model splits, our method jointly optimizes model initialization and dynamic model partitioning tailored to client latency and modality profiles, explicitly minimizing fine-tuning latency under real-time constraints. This enables practical deployment on highly heterogeneous, latency-sensitive edge environments with non-IID multi-modal data, a capability absent from all cited SOTA methods.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a privacy-preserving, fast-adapting federated meta-learning framework that explicitly models client-level data heterogeneity and temporal distribution shifts on resource-constrained wearable devices by integrating adaptive personalized model splitting with dynamic local-global knowledge distillation and differential privacy guarantees.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data suffers from client drift, temporal data distribution shifts, and resource constraints on edge devices, especially in wearable health monitoring where sensor noise and privacy are critical. Existing federated meta-learning and split learning methods either overlook temporal robustness, fail to jointly optimize for computational/communication efficiency under privacy constraints, or do not adequately handle client heterogeneity and non-IID data in evolving environments. This leads to degraded accuracy and privacy risks, limiting deployment in real-world, personalized health scenarios.

APPROACH:  
We propose a federated meta-learning system that (1) employs adaptive model splitting tailored per client device capability and communication conditions to minimize computation and communication overhead while maintaining privacy via differential privacy mechanisms; (2) integrates a dual-mode dynamic knowledge distillation process combining local drift-correcting distillation from historical global models and server-side distillation to robustly track temporal data shifts; and (3) incorporates a meta-learning adaptation layer that rapidly personalizes models to evolving sensor data streams via lightweight, privacy-preserving fine-tuning on-device within strict compute budgets.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark datasets: PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring, MHealth dataset, and WESAD (Wearable Stress and Affect Detection) – all featuring heterogeneous, temporally evolving, and noisy wearable sensor data. Baselines include FedGKD (Local-Global Knowledge Distillation), HeteroSFL (Split Federated Learning for Heterogeneous Clients), and MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning) adapted for federated settings. Metrics: achieve >3% absolute improvement in weighted F1-score for activity/stress classification, reduce per-round communication overhead by ≥25%, and enforce differential privacy with ε ≤ 1.0 while maintaining latency below 200 ms on representative ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller hardware. Conduct 5 random seeds runs, extensive ablation on model split granularity, distillation modes, and privacy budgets.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, this approach uniquely unifies adaptive model splitting with dynamic dual-mode knowledge distillation under strict differential privacy in a federated meta-learning context tailored for temporally evolving, heterogeneous wearable data. It explicitly addresses the temporal drift and computational/privacy constraints that existing methods like FedGKD or HeteroSFL do not simultaneously handle, enabling fast, robust personalization on resource-limited devices.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop Sparse Physics-Informed Spatiotemporal Neural Operators with Adaptive Multi-Node Communication Scheduling to enable scalable, uncertainty-aware, and transferable batch climate emulation across HPC clusters for coupled atmosphere-ocean simulations.

BACKGROUND:  
Current physics-informed neural operators (PINOs) with sparse connectivity have demonstrated reduced communication overhead and competitive accuracy for accelerating atmospheric climate model emulations (e.g., ICON). However, they do not explicitly handle coupled atmosphere-ocean interactions, uncertainty quantification, or dynamic communication scheduling to further improve scalability on HPC nodes. This limits their applicability for fully coupled Earth system batch climate predictions and uncertainty-aware ensemble forecasting.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel Sparse Spatiotemporal Neural Operator architecture that incorporates physics priors for both atmospheric and oceanic state variables and explicitly models their coupled dynamics. This is combined with an adaptive multi-node communication scheduling algorithm that dynamically exploits time-varying sparsity patterns and node locality to minimize cross-node synchronization during batch inference. Additionally, we integrate a Bayesian uncertainty quantification module based on variational dropout adapted for sparse operators to quantify predictive uncertainty. Training leverages distributed HPC clusters using ICON and CMIP6 coupled model outputs, with optimized communication-computation overlap to maximize throughput.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on ICON atmospheric benchmark data, CMIP6 coupled atmosphere-ocean model outputs (e.g., MPI-ESM1.2-LR and CESM2), and ERA5 reanalysis datasets for spatiotemporal climate state emulation. Baselines include Dense Physics-Informed Neural Operators (PINNs), Sparse Physics-Informed Neural Operators (SPINO), and state-of-the-art deep learning emulators like DeepSD and U-Net ensembles. Metrics include normalized RMSE (<1% target), communication overhead reduction (≥50% vs dense PINNs), wall-clock batch inference speedup (≥2x), and uncertainty calibration scores (e.g., Expected Calibration Error < 5%). Experiments run on an HPC cluster with 64 Nvidia A100 GPUs, with 5 random seeds per configuration and ablation studies on sparsity patterns, communication scheduling, and uncertainty modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior sparse PINO work limited to atmospheric-only models with static communication schemes, our method simultaneously models coupled atmosphere-ocean processes with explicit physics-informed sparsity, dynamically optimizes HPC node communication, and provides rigorous uncertainty quantification. This enables scalable, batch emulation of fully coupled climate models with quantifiable uncertainty, which existing methods cannot currently achieve.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a scalable Bayesian deep learning emulator framework that provides robust, calibrated uncertainty quantification for multi-decadal, large-ensemble climate model projections by integrating hierarchical temporal Bayesian inference with spatiotemporal attention mechanisms.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning climate emulators accelerate simulations but fail to reliably quantify uncertainty accumulation over long-term horizons and across large ensembles, limiting their utility for risk-averse climate policy decisions. Existing Bayesian methods either do not scale computationally or degrade in calibration quality for multi-decadal projections, preventing trustworthy ensemble uncertainty estimates critical for robust climate risk assessment.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical Bayesian deep neural network architecture combining temporal deep Gaussian processes with spatiotemporal attention modules to model climate emulator outputs over extended time horizons and ensemble members. Scalable variational inference techniques with structured temporal priors will enable efficient posterior approximation, capturing non-stationary uncertainty growth. The model will explicitly incorporate internal variability and scenario uncertainties, allowing end-to-end joint learning of emulator predictions and uncertainty bounds.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on CMIP6 decadal-to-century scale datasets including the SPEAR Large Ensemble, MPI-ESM1.2-LR ensemble datasets, and CORDEX regional downscaling outputs. Baselines include Deep Gaussian Processes (DGP), Bayesian LSTMs, and Deep Ensembles for climate emulation. Metrics will include Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) with target >10% reduction vs baselines, Expected Calibration Error (ECE) below 5%, and runtime speedup >5x over traditional Bayesian MCMC uncertainty quantification. Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 clusters with 5 seeds per setup and ablations on temporal prior structures and attention modules.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior climate emulators that either lack scalable Bayesian inference or do not address uncertainty accumulation over multi-decadal horizons and large ensembles, this work uniquely integrates hierarchical temporal Bayesian inference with spatiotemporal attention to maintain calibrated, tractable uncertainty quantification at scale, enabling actionable long-term climate risk analysis previously infeasible.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a sparse spatio-temporal basis-function deep learning emulator that efficiently reconstructs high-fidelity long-term climate projections from offline high-resolution datasets while explicitly modeling evolving climate variability and reducing computational complexity.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration struggle to capture the evolving, complex spatio-temporal dynamics inherent in long-term climate projections due to their high computational cost and inadequate sparse representation of climate patterns. Additionally, they often fail to generalize well over extended periods or changing climate regimes, limiting their utility for policy-relevant climate forecasting.

APPROACH: We propose designing a novel deep learning emulator architecture that incorporates learned sparse spatio-temporal basis functions coupled with adaptive reconstruction algorithms to model and compress offline high-resolution climate data efficiently. The method will employ a hybrid framework combining convolutional recurrent layers with sparse coding regularization to explicitly exploit spatio-temporal sparsity and evolving climate modes, enabling accurate, low-complexity emulation of long-term climate variability.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation will be conducted on the CMIP6 HighResMIP dataset, the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, and the CORDEX regional climate projections. Baselines for comparison include existing deep learning emulators such as DeepEmulator (2023), Bayesian U-Net for uncertainty quantification (2025), and linear pattern scaling methods. Metrics include mean absolute error (MAE) reduction by ≥10% over baselines for temperature and precipitation fields, ≥20% reduction in inference latency on NVIDIA A100 GPUs, and improved generalization measured by multi-year forecast skill scores across 5-year to 30-year horizons. Experiments will be run with at least 5 random seeds and include ablation studies isolating sparse coding effects.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior works that treat spatio-temporal data without enforcing sparsity or focus only on short- to medium-term emulation, our method uniquely learns adaptive sparse basis functions that explicitly model long-term climate variability and evolving patterns from offline high-res data, dramatically reducing computational cost while improving projection fidelity over extended periods. This capability to generalize and compress complex climate states offline for accelerated emulation is not achieved by current deep learning RCM emulators or linear scaling approaches.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a multi-objective hardware-in-the-loop Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework to discover energy-efficient, low-latency deep learning emulator architectures optimized for deployment on resource-constrained embedded climate sensors, enabling real-time, accurate climate model emulation at the edge.

BACKGROUND: Existing deep learning emulators for climate models are typically designed for powerful computing environments and fail to meet the strict energy, memory, and latency constraints of embedded systems used in in-situ climate sensors. This lack of tailored architectures limits continuous, localized climate data processing and adaptive monitoring capabilities in edge deployments. Current NAS approaches do not explicitly incorporate hardware feedback or jointly optimize emulator accuracy and embedded system constraints, creating a critical gap for embedded climate sensor applications.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-objective NAS framework that integrates real-time hardware-in-the-loop evaluations on target embedded platforms (e.g., ARM Cortex-M series and Nvidia Jetson Nano) to simultaneously optimize for emulator accuracy, energy consumption, and inference latency. The search space combines efficient convolutional and transformer-based building blocks designed for low-resource environments, while the objective function balances climate model emulation fidelity with embedded deployment constraints. To ensure emulator robustness, the framework leverages climate simulation transfer learning from high-fidelity datasets and incorporates uncertainty quantification modules.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark climate datasets—CORDEX regional climate projections, CMIP6 output subsets, and ERA5 reanalysis data—using baseline models such as U-Net, ConvLSTM, and DeepES (Deep Emulator for Earth Systems). Metrics include emulator accuracy (RMSE and spatial correlation with >3% improvement over baselines), inference latency (target <50 ms per sample), and energy consumption (measured via on-board power monitors, aiming for >40% reduction vs baselines). Experiments will run on embedded platforms with a fixed compute budget (48 GPU-hours for NAS), using 5 random seeds and ablation studies on search objectives and hardware feedback components.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior emulator models focused solely on accuracy or transferability, this work explicitly incorporates embedded hardware constraints into the NAS loop, producing architectures that meet stringent energy and latency targets without sacrificing emulator fidelity. This directly addresses the deployment bottleneck on edge climate sensors, enabling real-time, localized climate emulation unattainable by existing methods.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop an energy-constrained multi-objective Neural Architecture Search framework that incorporates uncertainty quantification modules to design deep learning climate emulators optimizing predictive accuracy, energy efficiency, and uncertainty calibration simultaneously on CMIP6 and CORDEX datasets.

BACKGROUND: Current neural architecture search (NAS) methods for climate emulators focus primarily on predictive accuracy and energy consumption, neglecting uncertainty quantification which is critical for reliable climate projections and decision-making. Additionally, existing deep learning emulators lack calibrated uncertainty estimates that reflect internal variability and model uncertainties inherent in climate data, limiting their practical deployment in climate impact assessments. There is a need for an integrated NAS approach that balances accuracy, energy efficiency, and uncertainty calibration for scalable, trustworthy climate emulators.

APPROACH: We propose an energy-aware multi-objective evolutionary NAS framework that jointly optimizes three metrics: root-mean-square error (RMSE) for emulator accuracy, total energy consumption during training and inference on HPC hardware, and uncertainty calibration measured by expected calibration error (ECE) and negative log-likelihood (NLL). The search space includes architectural modules designed to support Bayesian layers and quantile regression heads, enabling uncertainty quantification. The NAS will be guided by a Pareto front balancing accuracy, energy, and uncertainty calibration. Training and evaluation will be performed on CMIP6 historical and future scenario data, complemented by CORDEX regional downscaling datasets to ensure robustness and transferability.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate using CMIP6 historical runs, CMIP6 SSP2-4.5 future projections, and CORDEX RCM downscaled data; compare against baselines including Energy-constrained NAS (the tournament winner), U-Net with Bayesian layers (as in Uncertainty Quantification for Surface Ozone Emulators), and DeepSD for downscaling. Metrics: achieve a >30% reduction in training+inference energy vs Energy-constrained NAS baseline, RMSE within ±1% of best accuracy baseline, and improve uncertainty calibration by reducing ECE by 10–15%. Experimental protocol includes 3 independent runs per configuration on HPC clusters with NVIDIA A100 GPUs, 100 GPU-hours budget per NAS search, and ablation studies disabling uncertainty modules and energy constraints.

NOVELTY: This is the first climate emulator NAS that explicitly incorporates uncertainty quantification alongside accuracy and energy consumption as multi-objective objectives, addressing the crucial gap of trustworthiness in deep learning climate emulators which prior NAS methods and uncertainty-aware emulators have not jointly optimized for under realistic HPC constraints.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a meta-learning framework that enables rapid, patient-specific adaptation of uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks by learning a shared uncertainty-aware latent subspace for personalized healthcare diagnostics with minimal retraining.

BACKGROUND: Personalized healthcare diagnostics demand uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods that can quickly recalibrate on limited, heterogeneous patient data to inform critical clinical decisions. Existing UQ approaches either lack fast adaptation to new patients or require extensive retraining that is infeasible in clinical workflows, failing to generalize under strong inter-patient variability and data scarcity. This work addresses the open problem of enabling fast, reliable uncertainty estimation that adapts to individual patient data distributions without full model retraining.

APPROACH: We propose a meta-learning strategy that trains a deep neural network alongside an uncertainty-aware latent subspace representing patient-specific variations in uncertainty estimates. During meta-training on diverse patient cohorts, the model learns to map inputs to this latent subspace where uncertainty parameters can be rapidly fine-tuned using gradient updates on minimal patient data. Key technical components include (1) a probabilistic encoder-decoder architecture to extract and adapt latent uncertainty parameters, (2) integration of Bayesian meta-learning with active subspace identification to focus adaptation on critical uncertainty dimensions, and (3) a hybrid loss combining predictive accuracy and calibrated uncertainty metrics to encourage robust, well-calibrated estimates post-adaptation.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on at least three real-world personalized healthcare datasets with significant patient heterogeneity and limited per-patient samples, such as MIMIC-III for ICU patient time-series, UK Biobank imaging-genomics data, and the ChestX-ray14 dataset for radiological diagnosis. Baselines include Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs), Monte Carlo Dropout, Ensemble methods, and the Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC) method. Metrics: expected calibration error (ECE) reduction by >3%, negative log-likelihood (NLL) improvement by >5%, and adaptation speed measured as >50% reduction in retraining epochs/time to reach stable uncertainty estimates. The experimental protocol involves training on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~200 GPU-hours, running all experiments with 5 random seeds, and conducting ablations on the latent subspace dimensionality and meta-learning loss components.

NOVELTY: Unlike PSC and active subspace UQ methods that are static and require retraining for new data distributions, and unlike existing meta-learning UQ that does not incorporate explicit uncertainty latent subspaces, our approach jointly learns an uncertainty-aware latent subspace enabling rapid, patient-specific uncertainty adaptation with minimal retraining overhead. This solves the critical bottleneck of reliable, fast calibration under extreme data heterogeneity in personalized medical settings, which prior work cannot achieve.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an ensemble diversity maximization framework that dynamically integrates heterogeneous sensor models for autonomous vehicle sensor fusion, producing uncertainty estimates that explicitly model sensor noise and environmental variability under real-time constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current ensemble methods for uncertainty quantification (UQ) in autonomous vehicle sensor fusion inadequately capture model disagreement arising from heterogeneous sensor modalities (lidar, radar, cameras) and fail to incorporate sensor noise and dynamic environmental conditions into uncertainty estimates. This results in unreliable UQ that jeopardizes safety-critical decision-making under real-time constraints. The open problem is to design an ensemble approach that maximizes diversity across sensor-specific models while accurately reflecting both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in fused outputs within strict latency requirements.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel diversity maximization strategy that explicitly encourages heterogeneity in ensemble members by optimizing a custom loss combining disagreement measures across sensor modalities, sensor noise models, and environmental context embeddings derived from real-time data streams. The ensemble integrates probabilistic skip connections (PSC) to capture intermediate-layer uncertainty and incorporates dynamic weighting schemes that adjust contributions of each sensor-specific model based on real-time noise and environment estimates. A lightweight Bayesian active subspace component reduces the parameter space to accelerate inference without sacrificing uncertainty fidelity. This architecture jointly outputs fused predictions and calibrated uncertainty bounds, validated via Bhattacharyya coefficient-based inter-class confusion metrics.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark autonomous driving datasets with multimodal sensor data: KITTI (lidar + camera), nuScenes (lidar + radar + camera), and Waymo Open Dataset (full sensor suite). Baselines include Deep Ensembles, MC Dropout, Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC, 2025), and the active subspace method from 2023. Metrics: reduce Negative Log-Likelihood by >5%, improve Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by >10% absolute, and reduce latency by at least 25% compared to Deep Ensembles. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~2,000 GPU hours, averaging results over 5 random seeds, including ablations on diversity loss components and sensor weighting modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike PSC or active subspace methods that focus on single-model uncertainty or parameter reduction, this method directly optimizes ensemble diversity explicitly across heterogeneous sensor models under real-time constraints. It uniquely models both sensor noise and environmental variability dynamically within the ensemble weighting, producing uncertainty estimates aligned with real-world sensor fusion challenges not addressed by previous UQ approaches.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a compact, incrementally updatable generative latent space model optimized for mobile health monitoring devices that enables robust out-of-distribution detection and uncertainty quantification under streaming, noisy physiological data with constrained computational resources.

BACKGROUND:  
Mobile health monitoring requires continuous and reliable identification of anomalous physiological events (OOD detection) on resource-limited devices with intermittent connectivity. Existing generative latent space models for OOD detection are either too computationally heavy for on-device deployment or lack efficient incremental update mechanisms to adapt to evolving patient data streams, resulting in degraded uncertainty quantification and delayed anomaly detection.

APPROACH:  
We propose a lightweight variational autoencoder (VAE)-based latent space model integrated with an incremental Bayesian update scheme that updates the latent distribution parameters with streaming physiological inputs in real-time. The model incorporates a novel uncertainty calibration layer that leverages probabilistic skip connections (inspired by PSC) at intermediate latent representations to quantify epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty robustly. Efficient model pruning and quantization techniques are applied to ensure deployment on mobile platforms (e.g., ARM Cortex-M class processors). OOD detection is performed by evaluating the learned latent distribution divergence combined with Bhattacharyya coefficient-based inter-class overlap metrics to improve anomaly sensitivity without requiring retraining.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark physiological datasets—MIMIC-III Waveform Database, PhysioNet Challenge 2019, and CAP Sleep Database—comparing against baselines including Deep Ensemble models, MC Dropout applied to VAE architectures, and PSC-based uncertainty quantification. Metrics include OOD detection AUROC (>5% absolute improvement over baselines), latency reduction on-device by >30%, and uncertainty calibration error (expected calibration error, ECE) reduced by at least 10%. Experiments will run on embedded-class hardware simulators with 5 random seeds and include ablations on incremental update frequency and pruning levels.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either lacks incremental updating (e.g., classical VAE-based OOD detection) or cannot efficiently run on mobile devices with physiological streaming data, this method uniquely combines compact latent space modeling with incremental Bayesian updates and probabilistic skip connection uncertainty calibration optimized for constrained hardware. This results in timely, reliable uncertainty quantification and OOD detection in real-world mobile health scenarios that prior SOTA generative models and UQ techniques do not provide.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an efficient Bayesian Neural Network (BNN) approximation framework that leverages structured low-rank variational inference and adaptive active subspace projection to achieve real-time, reliable uncertainty quantification on resource-constrained edge devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Current Bayesian Neural Network methods provide theoretically grounded uncertainty estimates but incur prohibitive computational and memory costs, making them unsuitable for deployment on edge devices with stringent latency and resource constraints. Existing approaches either sacrifice uncertainty fidelity for efficiency or fail to scale down to real-time edge scenarios. This research targets the open problem of maintaining high-quality uncertainty quantification while drastically reducing inference overhead for trustworthy AI in IoT and mobile applications.

APPROACH:  
We propose a two-fold strategy combining (1) structured low-rank variational approximations of the BNN weight posterior to reduce parameter redundancy and memory footprint, and (2) automatic identification of active subspaces within the network parameter space that most influence uncertainty estimates, enabling targeted Bayesian updates only on these subspaces. We further integrate probabilistic skip connections at these active layers to enhance uncertainty calibration with minimal computational cost. This hybrid framework will employ lightweight Monte Carlo sampling restricted to the active subspace and optimized variational families, yielding uncertainty estimates with efficient edge-compatible runtime.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on CIFAR-10, Tiny ImageNet, and Cityscapes datasets under standard train/test splits, benchmarking against Monte Carlo Dropout, Bayes by Backprop, and Deep Ensembles baselines. Metrics include Expected Calibration Error (ECE) aiming for <3%, Negative Log Likelihood (NLL) improvements >5% absolute, and latency reduction targets of 40% compared to Bayes by Backprop on ARM Cortex-A72 CPUs. Conduct ablation studies on rank selection and active subspace dimensionality, with each result averaged over 5 random seeds under a fixed 48-hour GPU training budget and 1W power budget for inference simulations.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work such as “Learning Active Subspaces for Effective and Scalable UQ” (2023) that only identifies active subspaces without integrating them into a Bayesian framework, and “Probabilistic Skip Connections” (2025) that fit probabilistic models but do not optimize for edge constraints, our method uniquely combines structured Bayesian approximations with adaptive subspace projection and skip connections explicitly targeting resource-efficient, high-fidelity uncertainty quantification for edge deployment—a capability no existing method currently delivers.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate physics-informed neural operators with a decentralized Bayesian uncertainty quantification framework to jointly model sparse, asynchronous environmental sensor data streams under partial and evolving physical constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current environmental monitoring methods struggle to provide reliable uncertainty quantification (UQ) when sensor data are heterogeneous, noisy, sparse, and asynchronously collected, especially under incomplete or partially known physical models. Existing UQ approaches either ignore the decentralized nature of sensor networks or fail to incorporate evolving and incomplete physics, resulting in overconfident or inaccurate uncertainty estimates that undermine decision-making reliability.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid framework combining physics-informed neural operators (PINOs) to embed partial differential equation (PDE) constraints adaptively learned from data, with a decentralized Bayesian neural network (BNN) ensemble that propagates uncertainty in a distributed manner across sensor nodes. This framework explicitly models asynchronous sensor updates via time-aware variational inference and leverages active subspace identification to reduce dimensionality, enabling scalable and robust UQ under incomplete physical laws. The system dynamically updates physics constraints by incorporating discrepancies revealed in the sensor data, thus continuously refining both the physics model and uncertainty estimates.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the SensorScope dataset (outdoor environmental monitoring with sparse, asynchronous sensors), the Intel Lab Data (heterogeneous indoor environmental sensors), and the NOAA Buoy dataset (distributed oceanographic sensors with partial physics knowledge). Compare against baselines: Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC, 2025), Bayesian Neural Networks with Monte Carlo Dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016), and Physics-Informed Neural Networks for UQ (Raissi et al., 2020). Metrics include Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) improved by >3%, Calibration Error reduced by >15%, and Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) improved by >5% relative to the best baseline. Run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds per experiment; ablation studies will isolate physics constraint adaptation and decentralized inference components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike PSC and latent-space statistical inference methods that do not handle decentralized, asynchronous data or evolving physics, our method explicitly models partial and time-varying physical constraints within a distributed Bayesian framework, enabling accurate, well-calibrated UQ for large-scale, heterogeneous environmental sensor networks with sparse and noisy data streams.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a modality dropout-aware hierarchical foundation model that jointly learns scalable, unified embeddings for population-scale multimodal genomics and single-cell multimodal datasets, explicitly integrating missing modality patterns and cell-level heterogeneity to enable robust cross-resolution biological representation and imputation.

BACKGROUND: Current foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology struggle with missing data modalities and fail to integrate population-scale genomic variation with cellular heterogeneity in a unified framework. Existing approaches like “Scalable Batch Multimodal Alignment for Population Genomics with Missing Modalities” address missing modalities at the population scale but do not incorporate fine-grained single-cell multimodal data. There is a critical need for models that robustly align and impute across both bulk/population and single-cell modalities, facilitating integrated analysis spanning molecular scales and resolving biases caused by missing data or batch effects.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical multimodal foundation model architecture combining (1) a scalable batch multimodal alignment module with modality dropout-aware contrastive loss for population-level genomic and clinical modalities, and (2) a fine-grained single-cell multimodal encoder that jointly embeds transcriptomics, epigenomics, and spatial modalities. The model explicitly encodes missing modality masks at both population and single-cell levels and employs cross-resolution contrastive learning to align cellular embeddings to population embeddings. A modality dropout-aware imputation head reconstructs missing data at both scales. Training leverages distributed parallelism over UK Biobank genetic/clinical data and Human Cell Atlas single-cell multimodal data, enabling joint learning despite substantial missingness and heterogeneous batch effects.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on UK Biobank (genotype, phenotype, clinical data), Human Cell Atlas (multiome scRNA+ATAC), and Nephrobase Cell+ (kidney multimodal single-cell data) benchmarks. Baselines include Scalable Batch Multimodal Alignment, Nephrobase Cell+, and totalVI. Metrics: >20% improvement in imputation accuracy (AUROC/F1) for missing modalities; >5% absolute gain in embedding coherence (mean silhouette score) across modalities; and >10% improved transfer learning performance on downstream disease phenotype prediction and cell-type annotation tasks. Use 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, train for 5 epochs with 3 random seeds; conduct ablation studies on modality dropout rates, hierarchical contrastive losses, and imputation heads.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that separately models population-scale or single-cell multimodal data, this method integrates both scales within a unified hierarchical foundation model that explicitly conditions on missing modalities and batch heterogeneity across resolutions. It enables robust, scalable joint representation and imputation across thousands of individuals and millions of single cells, a capability absent from existing frameworks which either lack scalability, fine-grained cellular resolution, or explicit missing data modeling simultaneously.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a causal representation learning framework that explicitly models combinatorial perturbation effects in single-cell transcriptomic data using disentangled latent variables and structural causal constraints, enabling precise inference of direct and interaction effects in high-dimensional cellular profiles.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell foundation models fail to disentangle causal contributions of complex combinatorial perturbations due to confounding effects and nonlinear interactions, limiting mechanistic insight into cellular responses in drug screening and functional genomics. Existing methods do not adequately capture causal effect heterogeneity or interaction synergies from high-order perturbations in vitro, impeding reliable interpretation and prediction of cellular phenotypes.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel causal representation learning model that integrates (1) a structured latent variable architecture partitioning variation into independent causal factors corresponding to single and combinatorial perturbations, (2) a graph neural network to encode known perturbation-target biological pathways as structural causal constraints, and (3) counterfactual inference modules to simulate unseen perturbation combinations. The model jointly optimizes disentanglement and predictive accuracy using interventional single-cell RNA-seq data with combinatorial drug/genetic perturbations, enabling direct estimation of causal effects and their interactions at the single-cell level.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the Perturb-CITE-seq dataset (Norman et al., 2019), the MIX-Seq combinatorial drug screening dataset (Ben-David et al., 2020), and the sci-Plex 3 combinatorial CRISPR perturbation dataset (Srivatsan et al., 2021). Baselines include vanilla single-cell foundation models like scBERT, CPA (Compositional Perturbation Autoencoder), and GEARS. Metrics: achieve >3% absolute improvement in R2 for held-out perturbation effect prediction, >5% improvement in Average Causal Effect estimation accuracy, and reduce mean squared error (MSE) in counterfactual outcome prediction by at least 10%. Conduct ablations on latent disentanglement loss and causal graph constraints. Use 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for training with 5 random seeds per experiment.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior single-cell foundation models (e.g., scBERT, CPA) that lack explicit causal disentanglement and do not model combinatorial perturbation interactions structurally, this work uniquely integrates causal graphs with disentangled latent variables and counterfactual modules to precisely infer direct and interaction effects, enabling mechanistic interpretability and reliable out-of-distribution intervention predictions in complex combinatorial perturbation regimes.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Spatially Uncertainty-Aware Multimodal Foundation Model (SUM-FM) that integrates noisy spatial transcriptomics graphs with multi-omic single-cell data using hierarchical graph neural networks augmented by probabilistic spatial attention to robustly model cellular neighborhoods and their functional states.

BACKGROUND:  
Current graph neural network models for noisy single-cell spatial transcriptomics inadequately handle spatial measurement uncertainty and fail to integrate complementary multi-omic modalities, leading to suboptimal identification of cellular neighborhoods and biological interpretations. Existing approaches, including the winning Graph Neural Network with Spatial Attention, do not explicitly quantify spatial uncertainty in a principled probabilistic manner nor leverage joint embeddings from multimodal data such as single-cell ATAC-seq or proteomics, limiting their robustness and biological insight.

APPROACH:  
SUM-FM constructs hierarchical hybrid graphs combining spatial coordinates, gene expression, chromatin accessibility, and proteomic features at single-cell resolution. It models spatial uncertainty explicitly via a learned probabilistic spatial attention mechanism that weights edges by both spatial proximity and measurement confidence. The architecture uses a nested graph neural network scheme where intra-modal and inter-modal interactions are jointly learned through cross-modal message passing and uncertainty-aware attention layers. This foundation model is pretrained on large-scale multimodal spatial single-cell atlases and fine-tuned for downstream tasks such as neighborhood purity classification, spatial autocorrelation, and functional cell state prediction.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the 10x Visium Spatial Gene Expression, Slide-seqV2, and the Human Cell Atlas Multiome (joint scRNA + scATAC) datasets. Baselines include the Graph Neural Network with Spatial Attention (winning method), SpaGCN, and STAGATE. Metrics include neighborhood purity (aiming for >5% absolute improvement), Moran’s I spatial autocorrelation (>10% relative increase), and cell-type classification F1 (>3% absolute gain). Train on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 3 seeds per experiment; ablation studies will test the impact of (1) spatial uncertainty modeling, (2) multi-modal integration, and (3) hierarchical graph design.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior models, SUM-FM explicitly models spatial uncertainty through probabilistic attention rather than heuristic weighting, enabling principled denoising under noisy spatial measurements. It uniquely integrates multi-omic single-cell modalities into a unified hierarchical graph framework, surpassing single-modality spatial GNNs, and sets a new standard for biological interpretability and robustness in spatial single-cell foundation models.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an interactive symbolic-integrated foundation model platform that enables domain scientists to perform flexible, user-driven symbolic queries and iterative hypothesis refinement on multi-modal single-cell and genomics data without retraining, thereby accelerating biological discovery workflows.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell and genomics foundation models (e.g., Nephrobase Cell+, large-scale genomic language models) excel at embedding biological data but lack interactive interfaces supporting symbolic reasoning and real-time user-guided feedback. This limits their utility in research labs where hypothesis generation is iterative and requires interpretable, modular updates without costly full-model retraining. The open problem is enabling domain experts to seamlessly integrate symbolic biological knowledge into foundation models for flexible, iterative hypothesis refinement under realistic lab conditions.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture that integrates pretrained multimodal foundation models (language, graph, and vision) with a symbolic reasoning engine exposing queryable biological knowledge graphs and rule-based inference. Users interact via a natural language and symbolic query interface embedded within lab software to compose, refine, and visualize hypotheses on single-cell transcriptomics, epigenomics, and spatial data. Modular symbolic components and neural embeddings are updated independently through efficient fine-tuning and symbolic knowledge base extensions, enabling rapid iterative cycles without full model retraining.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on multi-modal datasets including Human Cell Atlas (HCA) v2023 single-cell RNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics data, ENCODE epigenomic datasets, and TCGA cancer genomics datasets. Baselines include Nephrobase Cell+, BioBERT, and scBERT for foundation embedding quality and GPT-4 powered lab assistants for interactive querying. Metrics: hypothesis validation accuracy (≥5% absolute improvement over baselines on held-out biological tasks), query response latency (<2 seconds), and user interaction efficiency measured by the number of iterative cycles to hypothesis convergence (≥20% reduction). Experimental protocol includes training on 8 A100 GPUs for up to 10 days, 5 random seeds, and ablation studies on symbolic vs neural-only components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work focusing solely on static embeddings or downstream prediction, this approach uniquely combines symbolic reasoning and foundation model embeddings into an interactive platform that supports real-time biological hypothesis generation and modular knowledge refinement without full retraining. It operationalizes human-in-the-loop scientific discovery workflows beyond current passive model use.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable, multimodal single-cell foundation model that jointly integrates spatial transcriptomics, histology images, and molecular graph data with a novel hierarchical graph neural network architecture to robustly model complex tissue microenvironments at unprecedented spatial and biological resolution.

BACKGROUND:  
Current graph neural network (GNN) approaches for single-cell spatial transcriptomics struggle with representing highly dense, irregularly sampled tissue microenvironments, leading to incomplete modeling of spatial cell-cell interactions and limited integration with complementary modalities such as histology images. There is a fundamental need for foundation models that unify molecular, spatial, and morphological data modalities to capture the full cellular context in health and disease with improved fidelity and interpretability.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical multimodal graph foundation model (HMG-FM) that constructs multi-scale spatial graphs by combining physical cell neighborhoods and tissue-level anatomical regions derived from histology. The model integrates spatial transcriptomics profiles with histological image embeddings extracted via a pretrained vision transformer and incorporates molecular interaction graphs (e.g., ligand-receptor networks) within a unified message-passing framework. Novel graph construction algorithms dynamically prune edges using adaptive density-aware criteria to maintain computational efficiency and biological relevance. Training leverages self-supervised objectives across modalities to learn robust joint representations that generalize across diverse tissue types and disease states.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be performed on the 10x Genomics Spatial Transcriptomics dataset (human breast cancer), the Human Cell Atlas Lung Spatial dataset, and the CosMx Spatial Molecular Imager dataset. Baselines include SpaGCN, STAGATE, Nephrobase Cell+, and a recent spatial graph transformer model. Metrics will quantify improvements in cell-type annotation accuracy (>3% absolute gain in F1 score), spatial interaction prediction AUPRC (>5% improvement), and spatial reconstruction fidelity (MSE reduction >10%). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~5,000 GPU hours, using 5 random seeds and ablations on modality integration, graph pruning, and hierarchical levels.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing models that treat spatial transcriptomics or histology independently or rely on flat graph representations, HMG-FM uniquely integrates multimodal data into a hierarchical graph framework that explicitly models tissue architecture and molecular interactions simultaneously. This enables accurate, scalable modeling of dense tissue microenvironments and cross-modal biological contexts that current state-of-the-art methods cannot capture.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Evolve multi-agent emergent communication protocols that explicitly optimize for symbolic interpretability, cross-domain generalization, and human-alignment by integrating multi-objective neuro-symbolic optimization with grounded human feedback on real-world task-oriented dialogue datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication methods in multi-agent systems focus primarily on task performance or efficiency but produce opaque protocols that fail to generalize across domains or support human interpretability. While interpretable symbolic protocol evolution has been demonstrated in virtual assistant collaborations, existing methods do not fully ground communication semantics in human-understandable concepts nor integrate multi-objective optimization with direct human feedback at scale. This limits the deployment of emergent protocols in heterogeneous multi-domain environments requiring both adaptability and transparency for user trust.

APPROACH:  
We propose a neuro-symbolic evolutionary framework that co-optimizes emergent communication protocols along three axes: (1) symbolic interpretability enforced via human-readable constraints and disentangled discrete representations, (2) cross-domain task generalization across multiple dialogue tasks, and (3) alignment with human communicative intent via iterative human-in-the-loop feedback. The framework evolves discrete communication vocabularies and compositional syntax trees using multi-objective evolutionary algorithms guided by automatic performance metrics and human annotation scores. Communication protocols are grounded in concept embeddings derived from pretrained language models fine-tuned on dialogue datasets, enabling semantic consistency across heterogeneous tasks.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the MultiWOZ 2.4, Schema-Guided Dialogue, and Taskmaster-1 datasets to benchmark cross-domain collaborative dialogue success rates, protocol interpretability using human annotation scores (e.g., >15% absolute improvement in interpretability rating vs baselines), and communication efficiency measured by message length and entropy reduction (targeting >10% compression vs baselines). Baselines include existing emergent communication methods: Differentiable Inter-Agent Communication (DIAL), Biases for Emergent Communication in MARL (Sukhbaatar et al., 2019), and the Interpretable Symbolic Protocol Evolution (ISPE) method from the winning tournament. Use 3 seeds per experiment, training on Nvidia A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~500 GPU-hours, and conduct ablation studies isolating symbolic constraints and human feedback impact.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, this approach explicitly integrates neuro-symbolic discrete protocol evolution with grounding in pretrained language embeddings and scalable human-in-the-loop interpretability feedback, enabling protocols that generalize strongly across multiple diverse dialogue domains while maintaining human-comprehensible semantics. Prior emergent communication studies lack this combined focus on multi-domain generalization with direct human-alignment via symbolic constraints, which is critical for practical, trustworthy multi-agent communication deployment.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate hierarchical symbolic logic constraints with neural protocol synthesis to enable dynamically adaptive, semantically consistent emergent communication protocols that maintain logical interpretability under domain shifts in multi-agent scientific knowledge exchange.

BACKGROUND:  
Current neural emergent communication protocols lack robust guarantees of logical consistency and semantic interpretability when facing evolving terminologies or domain shifts, leading to miscommunication and erroneous inferences in distributed scientific multi-agent systems. Existing approaches like logic-constrained neural protocol synthesis enforce static logical consistency but fail to adapt constraints dynamically as underlying knowledge bases evolve. This gap hinders reliable long-term multi-agent collaboration in scientific environments where ontologies and vocabularies continuously change.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel framework that combines hierarchical symbolic logic constraints, represented as modular, updateable logic programs, with neural message generation policies trained via reinforcement learning. The hierarchical logic constraints encode domain knowledge at multiple abstraction levels, enabling dynamic adjustment of the communication protocol’s logical rules in response to ontology updates or semantic drift. Neural policies learn to generate messages consistent with the currently active logical layer, enforced via constrained optimization during training. The system includes an interpretable logic module interface to monitor and modify constraints in real time, supporting continuous adaptation and provable semantic consistency despite evolving terminology.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on BioOntology Exchange and UMLS Metathesaurus datasets to simulate evolving biomedical terminologies, and on the CLEVR-CoGenT dataset to test compositional communication under varying object attribute distributions. Baselines include the original Logic-Constrained Neural Protocol Synthesis, Deep Communicating Agents (DCA), and Neural Belief Propagation methods. Metrics include semantic error rate reduction (>30% improvement targeted over original logic-constrained methods), communication interpretability scores (measured via human expert annotation on message clarity), and task success rate under domain shifts (>10% absolute gain). Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a training budget of 72 hours per configuration, with 5 random seeds each and ablation studies on hierarchical logic depth and constraint update frequency.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that enforces static logical constraints (Logic-Constrained Neural Protocol Synthesis) or purely neural emergent protocols lacking logical guarantees, this method uniquely enables *dynamic adaptation* of interpretable logic constraints within emergent communication, ensuring provable semantic consistency under domain evolution—a capability absent from existing neuro-symbolic multi-agent communication frameworks.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a real-time adaptive symbolic-neural emergent communication protocol for multi-agent systems that dynamically modulates communication modality and token complexity based on environmental context and agent roles, improving coordination efficiency and interpretability under strict latency constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication methods for multi-agent coordination, including symbolic-neural hybrids like the Real-time Symbolic-Neural Hybrid Protocol for Autonomous Vehicle Coordination, either apply a fixed communication modality or fail to adapt communication complexity dynamically to changing task demands and environmental uncertainty. This rigidity limits responsiveness in highly dynamic, safety-critical scenarios such as autonomous vehicle coordination or multi-robot navigation, where latency and interpretability trade-offs must be balanced in real time. The open problem is enabling agents to flexibly and autonomously select between neural and symbolic communication representations on-the-fly, optimizing both decision latency and communication clarity under varying environmental and task constraints.

APPROACH:  
We propose an adaptive communication framework where each agent learns a context-aware gating mechanism that selects and fuses symbolic tokens or continuous neural embeddings based on real-time assessments of environmental complexity, task urgency, and role-specific communication demands. The system uses a lightweight meta-controller trained via multi-agent reinforcement learning with latency and interpretability rewards to dynamically adjust the granularity and modality of messages (e.g., switching from compact symbolic tokens to richer neural vectors or hybrid forms). A differentiable symbolic grounding module enforces traffic or task rules, ensuring interpretability, while a neural module encodes environment-dependent nuances. Communication overhead and decision latency are jointly optimized with a multi-objective loss. Validation occurs in both autonomous driving (CARLA simulator) and decentralized multi-robot coordination tasks (Google Research Football) to demonstrate cross-domain generality.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on CARLA Autonomous Driving Benchmark, Google Research Football 2v2 and 5v5 multi-agent scenarios, and the OpenAI Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) for navigation. Baselines include the Real-time Symbolic-Neural Hybrid Protocol (from the tournament winner), Neuro-spike Communication (from 2025), and Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient with Emergent Communication (MADDPG-Comm). Metrics include average decision latency (targeting <75ms, a 25% reduction vs baseline), communication interpretability score (measured via symbolic token reconstruction accuracy, aiming >90%), and task success rate or collision avoidance rate (improving by at least 5% absolute). Experiments run on 8 V100 GPUs with 5 random seeds, including ablations on gating mechanism effectiveness and communication modality fusion.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that uses fixed or static symbolic-neural fusions, our method is the first to enable dynamic, learned switching and blending between symbolic and neural communication modalities conditioned on real-time environmental and task context, achieving substantially lower latency without sacrificing interpretability or safety. Prior adaptive emergent communication approaches do not explicitly enforce symbolic grounding for interpretability under latency constraints, nor do they demonstrate cross-domain validation in both driving and multi-robot coordination scenarios.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop a noise-robust, compositional emergent symbolic communication protocol for multi-agent robotic systems that enforces compactness and abstraction explicitly via structured message regularization and error-correcting codes, enabling reliable coordination under intermittent, lossy, and bandwidth-constrained communication.

BACKGROUND: Current emergent communication systems for multi-agent robotics fail to maintain compositionality and meaning when communication is sparse, intermittent, or noisy, leading to mission-critical coordination failures in hazardous environments. Specifically, prior approaches lack mechanisms to enforce message abstraction and compactness simultaneously with robustness to message loss or corruption, which is essential for deploying distributed robot teams in real-world disaster-response or exploration scenarios.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-component framework combining (1) a structured latent space regularization that enforces compositionality by factorizing messages into disentangled symbolic subunits, (2) integrated lightweight error-correcting codes embedded within emergent messages to tolerate noise and packet loss, and (3) a communication budget constraint module that optimizes message compactness via differentiable compression losses.
